w_5 S_5 &= 0.15 \times 54 = 8.10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Total weighted significance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{tot} &= 21.60 + 18.00 + 3.00 + 9.80 + 8.10 = 60.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** As per threshold, $40 < 60.50 \le 70$ → **Conditional Approval** subject to strict EMP.

---

#### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** Discuss the Leopold Matrix method of impact identification. State its merits and limitations in industrial EIA. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermal power plant emits $\mathrm{SO_2}$ at **$Q = 800\ \mathrm{g/s}$** from a stack of effective height **$H = 100\ \mathrm{m}$**. Wind speed **$u = 5\ \mathrm{m/s}$**. Pasquill stability D, downwind distance **$x = 3000\ \mathrm{m}$**, centreline ($y=0$). Compute the ground-level concentration using the Gaussian plume formula and compare with the NAAQS 24-hr standard of **$\mathbf{80\ \mu g/m^3}$**. **(7 marks)**

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

The **Leopold Matrix** is a two-dimensional checklist developed by Luna Leopold et al. (1971). The **rows** list **100 project actions** (e.g., land clearing, blasting, stack emissions) and **columns** list **88 environmental characteristics** (physical, chemical, biological, socio-economic).

Each intersection cell receives:
- A **magnitude** score (1–10).
- An **importance/sensitivity** score (1–10).
- A slash notation (e.g., 8/9) is used.

**Merits:**
- Comprehensive coverage
- Identifies secondary and tertiary impacts
- Easy for interdisciplinary teams

**Limitations:**
- Subjective scoring
- No temporal dimension
- No spatial precision
- 8800 cells — tedious for large projects

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1:** Compute dispersion coefficients at $x = 3000\ \mathrm{m}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_y &= 0.08 \times 3000 \times (1 + 0.0001 \times 3000)^{-1/2} \\
        &= 240 \times (1.3)^{-0.5} \\
        &= 240 \times 0.8771 \\
        &= 210.5\ \mathrm{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_z &= 0.06 \times 3000 \times (1 + 0.0015 \times 3000)^{-1/2} \\
        &= 180 \times (5.5)^{-0.5} \\
        &= 180 \times 0.4264 \\
        &= 76.75\ \mathrm{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Gaussian formula on centreline.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(x,0,0) &= \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2 \sigma_z^2}\right) \\
         &= \frac{800}{\pi \times 5 \times 210.5 \times 76.75} \times \exp\!\left(-\frac{100^2}{2 \times 76.75^2}\right) \\
         &= \frac{800}{253{,}734.7} \times \exp\!\left(-\frac{10{,}000}{11{,}781.1}\right) \\
         &= 3.153 \times 10^{-3} \times \exp(-0.8489) \\
         &= 3.153 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.4277 \\
         &= 1.349 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{g/m^3} \\
         &= 1349\ \mu\mathrm{g/m^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with NAAQS.

$1349\ \mu\mathrm{g/m^3} \gg 80\ \mu\mathrm{g/m^3}$ → **Violation by a factor of ~16.86**.

**Recommendation:** Install Flue-Gas Desulphurisation (FGD), raise effective stack height, or relocate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Many students forget the **exponential term** $\exp(-H^2 / 2\sigma_z^2)$ in the Gaussian formula. Without it, the answer becomes **overestimated by 2–3×** and loses 2 marks.
> - Always state the **units explicitly** ($\mu\mathrm{g/m^3}$ vs $\mathrm{g/m^3}$) — examiners deduct marks for missing unit conversion.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **EA** = broad term; **EIA** = project-specific; **SEA** = policy/plan-level.
- EIA in India is governed by **EIA Notification 2006** (and 2020 amendment) under **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**.
- **Eight stages:** Screening → Scoping → Baseline → Impact → Mitigation → EMP → Public Hearing → Monitoring.
- Industrial projects classified **A** (Central) or **B** (State); **B1** needs full EIA, **B2** only EMP.
- Significance formula: $S = M \times V$; Weighted total: $S_{tot} = \sum w_i S_i$.
- Mitigated impact: $S_{mit} = S_{org}(1 - \eta)$.
- Gaussian plume is the standard tool for atmospheric impact in industries.
- Critical water body formula: $C_{max} = \dfrac{C_s Q_s + C_e Q_e}{Q_s + Q_e}$.
- **Carbon footprint** must be quantified; afforestation area = $CF / \text{sequestration rate}$.
- **Leopold Matrix:** 100 actions × 88 factors; subjective scoring; no spatial dimension.
- Always state **threshold values and decision logic** in your answer — examiners love visible decision flow.
- **Common pitfall:** Not stating units; misclassifying SEA vs EIA; skipping public hearing step.
- **Threshold band (typical):** <40 Approve · 40–70 Conditional · >70 Reject.
- Industry examples to memorise: Thermal (>500 MW), Refineries, Cement (>1 MTPA), Mining (>5 MTPA), Iron & Steel (>0.8 MTPA) — all Category A.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waste Audits and Pollution Prevention Opportunities in Textile
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT831 - ENVIRONMENT MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Waste Audits and Pollution Prevention Opportunities in Textile

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Waste Audit — Formal Definition

A **Waste Audit** is a systematic, documented, and quantitative procedure used to identify, characterize, measure, and evaluate the quantity and composition of solid and liquid waste streams generated across an industrial process, with the ultimate objective of identifying opportunities for source reduction, reuse, recycling, and treatment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> A waste audit in the textile industry is a structured assessment of fibre, chemical, water, and energy losses occurring at each stage of textile processing (fibre preparation → spinning → weaving → wet processing → finishing) so that pollution prevention opportunities can be prioritized using mass-balance techniques.

## 1.2 Pollution Prevention (P2) — Formal Definition

**Pollution Prevention (P2)** is the engineering and management practice of reducing or eliminating pollutants at the source, before they are generated, rather than managing them after creation ("end-of-pipe" treatment). It is the highest-ranked strategy in the **Environmental Management Hierarchy**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Environmental Management Hierarchy (KTU 2024 — Environmental Management Systems):**
> **Most Preferred** ➜ **Source Reduction (P2)**
> ➜ **Recycling / Reuse (in-process)**
> ➜ **Treatment (end-of-pipe)**
> ➜ **Disposal** **➜ Least Preferred**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Leaking Tap and the Bucket"**
> Imagine a textile dyeing unit as a kitchen sink where coloured water (dyebath effluent) flows continuously into a bucket (treatment plant).
> - **End-of-Pipe Approach** = Letting the bucket overflow and mopping the floor afterwards. You solve the *symptom*.
> - **Pollution Prevention Approach** = Fixing the leaking tap (using a high-fixation reactive dye so less colour goes down the drain) and turning off the tap when not needed (right-first-time dyeing).
>
> **A Waste Audit** is like calling a plumber with a flow-meter — he measures exactly *how much* water is leaking, *from where*, and *at what rate*, so you know which tap to repair first.

## 1.4 Why Textile Industry Demands Special Focus

The textile industry is consistently ranked among the **top three most polluting industrial sectors globally**. Key statistics relevant to KTU valuation:

| Metric | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Water consumption for cotton dyeing | **80–150 L per kg of fabric** |
| Number of distinct wet-process stages | **8–10** |
| Global share of industrial water pollution from textiles | **~20 %** |
| Reactive dye fixation efficiency (conventional) | **60–70 %** (rest is waste) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single kg of textile can generate up to **200 L** of highly coloured effluent containing residual dyes, salts, alkalis, surfactants, and heavy-metal complex mordants — making waste auditing and P2 economically and ecologically critical.

## 1.5 Scope of the Module-3 Topic

This module covers:
1. **Types of waste audits** (preliminary vs detailed)
2. **Methodology** of conducting a textile waste audit
3. **Mass-balance approach** to quantify losses
4. **Source-reduction techniques** specific to textile wet processing
5. **Case studies** of P2 implementation in cotton, wool, and synthetic processing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Waste Audits

### 2.1.1 Preliminary Waste Audit (Walk-Through Audit)
- Visual inspection of each processing unit.
- Identifies obvious waste streams, storage issues, and housekeeping losses.
- Duration: **1–3 days**.
- Output: Qualitative list of waste sources and rough estimates.

### 2.1.2 Detailed (Quantitative) Waste Audit
- Involves sampling and laboratory analysis of each stream.
- Uses **flow meters**, weighing, and chemical analysis.
- Duration: **2–6 weeks**.
- Output: Quantitative mass balance, pollution load, and P2 opportunity matrix.

### 2.1.3 Pre-Investment / Pre-Design Audit
- Conducted before installing new equipment or expanding capacity.
- Establishes baseline and design criteria for P2 measures.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Methodology of a Textile Waste Audit

1. **Planning & Goal Definition**
   - Define audit boundary (gate-to-gate vs cradle-to-gate).
   - Identify process steps to be audited.
2. **Process Mapping (Flow Charting)**
   - Document each input: fibre, dye, chemical, water, energy.
   - Document each output: product, effluent, solid waste, air emission.
3. **Data Collection**
   - Flow measurements (water, effluent) at each stage.
   - Mass measurements (chemical dosing, fabric weight before/after).
   - Operational logs (temperature, time, batch size).
4. **Sampling & Analysis**
   - Composite sampling of each effluent stream.
   - Analysis: pH, COD, BOD, TSS, TDS, colour (ADMI units), residual dye, salinity.
5. **Mass Balance Construction**
   - Inputs = Outputs + Accumulation + Losses.
6. **Identification of P2 Opportunities**
   - Rank opportunities by feasibility, cost, and environmental benefit.
7. **Reporting & Recommendation**
   - Executive summary, detailed findings, prioritized P2 action plan.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this order matters:** Without a process map (Step 2), sampling locations cannot be chosen correctly. Without mass balance (Step 5), no P2 measure can be quantitatively justified to management — KTU examiners specifically look for this sequence in 14-mark questions.

## 2.3 Pollution Prevention Opportunities in Textile Wet Processing

The textile wet-processing sequence (the **heart of textile pollution**) is:

> **Desizing → Scouring → Bleaching → Mercerizing → Dyeing → Printing → Finishing → Washing (after each step)**

### 2.3.1 P2 by Process Modification

| Stage | Conventional Issue | P2 Opportunity |
|---|---|---|
| Desizing | Large volumes of hot water | **Enzymatic desizing** (low temp, biodegradable effluent) |
| Scouring | High alkali, high COD | **Bio-scouring using pectinases** |
| Bleaching | Chlorine bleach → AOX | **H₂O₂ bleaching** with stabilizers |
| Mercerizing | High NaOH loss | **Caustic recovery by membrane filtration** |
| Dyeing | 30–40 % dye unfixed | **Right-First-Time (RFT) dyeing**; high-fixation reactive dyes (≥ 90 %) |
| Printing | Wash-water rich in thickeners | **Digital printing** (waterless) |
| Finishing | Solvent emissions | **Water-based finishes**; **plasma treatment** |

### 2.3.2 P2 by Water & Energy Conservation

- **Counter-current washing**: water flows opposite to fabric direction, reducing fresh water demand by **50–70 %**.
- **Heat recovery from hot effluent**: plate heat exchangers recover **60–80 %** of thermal energy.
- **Low liquor ratio dyeing machines**: liquor ratio 1:4 instead of 1:10 → water savings of **60 %**.
- **Reuse of process water**: rinsing water of one step reused for less-critical previous step.

### 2.3.3 P2 by Chemical Substitution

- Replace **metal-complex acid dyes** with reactive or natural dyes.
- Replace **salt (NaCl) in reactive dyeing** with cationic pre-treatment of cotton (salt-free dyeing).
- Replace **PVA sizing** with **starch-based sizing** (biodegradable).
- Use **low-temperature plasmas** for surface modification in place of wet-chemical treatments.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Material Balance | $\text{Input} = \text{Output} + \text{Accumulation} + \text{Loss}$ | kg or mol | Quantify unfixed dye / chemical loss |
| 2 | Pollution Load (mass-based) | $PL = Q \times C$ | kg/day | Where $Q$ = flow (m³/day), $C$ = concentration (kg/m³) |
| 3 | Specific Water Consumption | $SWC = \dfrac{V_{\text{water}}}{M_{\text{fabric}}}$ | L/kg | Water-use efficiency KPI |
| 4 | Dye Fixation Efficiency | $\eta_F = \dfrac{D_{\text{fixed}}}{D_{\text{applied}}} \times 100$ | % | Measures dyeing performance |
| 5 | Reduction Efficiency of P2 | $\%R = \dfrac{C_{\text{before}} - C_{\text{after}}}{C_{\text{before}}} \times 100$ | % | Quantifies P2 benefit |
| 6 | Process Mass Intensity | $PMI = \dfrac{\text{Total mass input (incl. water)}}{\text{Mass of product}}$ | dimensionless | Green chemistry KPI |
| 7 | E-factor (Environmental factor) | $E = \dfrac{\text{Mass of waste}}{\text{Mass of product}}$ | kg waste / kg product | P2 evaluation metric |
| 8 | COD Mass Loading | $COD_L = Q \times COD_{\text{conc}}$ | kg O₂/day | ETP design parameter |
| 9 | Liquor Ratio | $LR = \dfrac{V_{\text{liquor}}}{M_{\text{fabric}}}$ | dimensionless | Dyeing efficiency |
| 10 | Salt Savings Index | $\Delta S = S_{\text{conv}} - S_{\text{P2}}$ | g/L | P2 measure evaluation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU Valuation:** Always state units explicitly. The most common mark-deduction occurs when students write "$Q \times C$" without substituting the units of $Q$ (m³/day) and $C$ (mg/L or kg/m³) — examiners expect correct dimensional consistency.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Engineering Design**: Waste audit data drive the design of Effluent Treatment Plants (ETP) and in-process recovery systems.
- **Regulatory Compliance**: Indian Pollution Control Boards (KSPCB/CPCB) require quantitative waste audits for consent under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** and **Hazardous Waste Management Rules**.
- **Economic Gains**: P2 measures in textile wet processing typically yield **payback periods of 6–24 months** by recovering chemicals, water, and energy.
- **Certification Support**: Forms the empirical backbone for **ISO 14001 (Environmental Management Systems)** and **ZLD (Zero Liquid Discharge)** compliance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Material Balance Across a Dyeing Process

Consider a single reactive-dyeing batch for cotton fabric.

**Given:**
- Fabric mass: $M_f = 100$ kg
- Liquor ratio: $LR = 1{:}8$ → Volume of dyebath $V = 800$ L
- Dye applied: $D_{\text{applied}} = 5$ kg
- Salt (NaCl) applied: $S_{\text{applied}} = 50$ kg
- Soda ash (fixation alkali): $A_{\text{applied}} = 10$ kg

**Conventional (uncontrolled) reactive dyeing:**
- Dye fixation efficiency $\eta_F = 65$ % → unfixed dye $= 35$ % of $5 = 1.75$ kg
- Salt hydrolysis loss (to effluent): $S_{\text{eff}} = 40$ kg
- Effluent volume after dye + rinse: $V_{\text{eff}} = 1600$ L

**Step 1: Mass balance on dye**

$$
D_{\text{applied}} = D_{\text{fixed}} + D_{\text{effluent}} + D_{\text{loss,other}}
$$

$$
5\ \text{kg} = (5 \times 0.65)\ \text{kg} + D_{\text{effluent}} + 0
$$

$$
D_{\text{effluent}} = 1.75\ \text{kg per batch}
$$

**Step 2: Compute dye concentration in effluent**

$$
C_{\text{dye}} = \dfrac{D_{\text{effluent}}}{V_{\text{eff}}} = \dfrac{1.75\ \text{kg}}{1600\ \text{L}} = 1.094 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/L}
$$

$$
C_{\text{dye}} = 1094\ \text{mg/L}
$$

**Step 3: Pollution Load (PL) per day (assume 2 batches/day)**

$$
Q_{\text{daily}} = 2 \times 1600\ \text{L/day} = 3200\ \text{L/day} = 3.2\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

$$
PL_{\text{dye}} = Q \times C = 3.2\ \text{m}^3/\text{day} \times 1.094\ \text{kg/m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{PL_{\text{dye}} = 3.50\ \text{kg dye per day}}
$$

**Step 4: Salt pollution load**

$$
PL_{\text{salt}} = 2 \times 40 = 80\ \text{kg NaCl/day}
$$

**Step 5: Specific Water Consumption (SWC)**

$$
SWC = \dfrac{V_{\text{daily water}}}{M_{\text{fabric,daily}}} = \dfrac{2 \times (800 + 800)\ \text{L}}{2 \times 100\ \text{kg}} = 16\ \text{L/kg}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **exact format** examiners expect in a 14-mark Part B answer. Showing every algebraic step with text justification is what secures full marks.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: P2 Impact Comparison

### Scenario A — Conventional Dyeing (baseline)
- $D_{\text{eff}} = 1.75$ kg/batch; $PL_{\text{dye}} = 3.50$ kg/day
- $S_{\text{eff}} = 40$ kg/batch; $PL_{\text{salt}} = 80$ kg/day
- $V_{\text{eff}} = 1600$ L/batch; $SWC = 16$ L/kg

### Scenario B — P2 Implementation (high-fixation reactive dye + counter-current wash)

Improvements:
- Dye fixation rises from **65 % to 92 %**
- Salt reduced by **50 %** using cationic cotton pre-treatment
- Effluent volume reduced by **40 %** via counter-current wash

| Metric | Conventional (A) | After P2 (B) | Reduction (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\eta_F$ | 65 % | 92 % | +27 (relative) |
| $D_{\text{eff}}$ (kg/batch) | 1.75 | 0.40 | **77.1 %** |
| $PL_{\text{dye}}$ (kg/day) | 3.50 | 0.80 | **77.1 %** |
| $S_{\text{eff}}$ (kg/batch) | 40 | 20 | **50 %** |
| $PL_{\text{salt}}$ (kg/day) | 80 | 40 | **50 %** |
| $V_{\text{eff}}$ (L/batch) | 1600 | 960 | **40 %** |
| $SWC$ (L/kg) | 16 | 9.6 | **40 %** |

### Computation of Reduction Efficiency (Verification)

$$
\%R_{\text{dye}} = \dfrac{1.75 - 0.40}{1.75} \times 100 = 77.14\ \% \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
\%R_{\text{salt}} = \dfrac{40 - 20}{40} \times 100 = 50\ \% \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
\%R_{\text{water}} = \dfrac{1600 - 960}{1600} \times 100 = 40\ \% \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-step logic for the matrix:** For each row, identify which *physical principle* drives the improvement. For dye → better chemistry (higher fixation); for salt → cationic pre-treatment removes need for electrolyte; for water → counter-current flow reduces fresh water demand. KTU examiners expect students to **explain the "why"**, not just quote the number.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cost-Benefit Justification of a P2 Measure

**Sample case:** Installing a counter-current wash system.

- Capital cost of retrofit: $\text{CapEx} = ₹15{,}00{,}000$
- Annual water savings: $V_{\text{saved}} = 20{,}000\ \text{m}^3/\text{year}$
- Cost of fresh water + effluent treatment: ₹40/m³
- Annual O&M cost increase: ₹1,00,000/year

**Annual Savings:**

$$
\text{Savings} = 20{,}000\ \text{m}^3/\text{year} \times ₹40/\text{m}^3 = ₹8{,}00{,}000/\text{year}
$$

**Net Annual Benefit (NAB):**

$$
NAB = \text{Savings} - \text{O\&M} = 8{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹7{,}00{,}000/\text{year}
$$

**Simple Payback Period (SPP):**

$$
SPP = \dfrac{\text{CapEx}}{NAB} = \dfrac{15{,}00{,}000}{7{,}00{,}000} = 2.14\ \text{years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU answers:** Quantitative economic justification elevates a Part-B answer from descriptive to analytical — this is what distinguishes a 10-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Step-by-Step Textile Waste Audit Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage A: Planning and Boundary Definition] --> B[Stage B: Process Flow Mapping]
    B --> C[Stage C: Data and Flow Measurement]
    C --> D[Stage D: Sampling and Laboratory Analysis]
    D --> E[Stage E: Mass Balance Construction]
    E --> F[Stage F: Identify P2 Opportunities]
    F --> G[Stage G: Ranking by Feasibility and Cost]
    G --> H[Stage H: Report and Recommendations]
    H --> I[Implementation of P2 Measures]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** Each rectangular node represents one audit stage. The arrow flow mirrors the chronological order a textile engineer follows. Skip any step and the audit loses validity.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Textile Wet Processing — Waste-Generating Stages and P2 Levers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Raw Grey Fabric] --> P1[Desizing]
    P1 --> P2[Scouring]
    P2 --> P3[Bleaching]
    P3 --> P4[Mercerizing]
    P4 --> P5[Dyeing]
    P5 --> P6[Printing]
    P6 --> P7[Finishing]
    P7 --> P8[Finished Fabric]
    W1[High COD, Size Eff] -.->|Enzymatic Desizing| P1
    W2[Alkali Effluent] -.->|Bio-Scouring| P2
    W3[Chlorinated AOX] -.->|H2O2 Bleach| P3
    W4[Caustic Waste] -.->|Membrane Recovery| P4
    W5[Unfixed Dye + Salt] -.->|RFT Dyeing| P5
    W6[Thickener Wash] -.->|Digital Printing| P6
    W7[Solvent VOC] -.->|Water Finish| P7
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read:** The upper horizontal flow represents the textile process. Each dashed arrow from a "waste node" (W1–W7) points to the corresponding process stage and labels the **P2 intervention** that mitigates that specific waste.

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Pollution Prevention Hierarchy in Textile Industry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Most Preferred: Source Reduction] --> L2[Recycling and Reuse in Process]
    L2 --> L3[Treatment End of Pipe ETP]
    L3 --> L4[Disposal Least Preferred]
    L1 --> S1[Examples: RFT Dyeing, Low LR, High Fixation Dyes]
    L1 --> S2[Examples: Enzymatic Processing, Plasma Treatment]
    L2 --> S3[Examples: Counter Current Wash, Heat Recovery]
    L2 --> S4[Examples: Caustic Recovery, Dye Bath Reuse]
    L3 --> S5[Examples: Activated Sludge, Membrane Bioreactor]
    L4 --> S6[Examples: Landfill, Incineration]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture — P2 Decision Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Audit Baseline Established] --> B{Q1: Does change reduce raw material use?}
    B -- Yes --> C[High Priority Source Reduction]
    B -- No --> D{Q2: Does change enable in process reuse?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Medium Priority Reuse Recycle]
    D -- No --> F{Q3: Does change reduce effluent toxicity?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Treatment Stage Modification]
    F -- No --> H[Reject Measure No P2 Benefit]
    C --> I[Implement and Monitor]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    I --> J[Verify Mass Balance and Report]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define waste audit. List the key steps involved in conducting a textile waste audit. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **waste audit** is a systematic procedure to identify, quantify, and characterize waste streams generated in an industrial process, enabling the formulation of pollution prevention and resource recovery strategies.

**Key steps (1 mark each):**
1. **Planning & Boundary Definition** — defining the scope (e.g., gate-to-gate).
2. **Process Flow Mapping** — charting all inputs (fibres, dyes, chemicals, water) and outputs.
3. **Data Collection & Sampling** — flow measurement and laboratory analysis of effluent parameters (COD, BOD, TSS, colour, residual dye).
4. **Mass Balance Construction** — applying $\text{Input} = \text{Output} + \text{Losses}$.
5. **Identification & Ranking of P2 Opportunities** — by feasibility, cost, and environmental benefit.

---

### Question 2: What is pollution prevention (P2)? How does it differ from end-of-pipe treatment? `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Pollution Prevention (P2)** is the practice of reducing or eliminating the generation of pollutants at their source, rather than treating them after they have been formed.

| Aspect | Pollution Prevention | End-of-Pipe Treatment |
|---|---|---|
| Stage of intervention | At source (before pollution forms) | After pollution is generated |
| Hierarchy rank | **Highest (Most preferred)** | Lower than P2 and reuse |
| Approach | Process modification, chemical substitution, good housekeeping | Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP), filters, biological units |
| Examples in textile | RFT dyeing, enzymatic desizing, counter-current wash | Activated sludge, membrane bioreactor |
| Cost-effectiveness | Long-term savings | Continuous O&M cost |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B carries **14 marks** and typically has internal choice. Each answer is divided into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain with a neat flow diagram, the step-by-step procedure of conducting a waste audit in a textile wet-processing industry. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A textile waste audit follows a structured eight-stage procedure:

**Stage 1 — Planning & Boundary Setting:** Define the audit scope (e.g., from greige fabric entry to finished fabric dispatch). Identify the team, duration, and resources.

**Stage 2 — Process Flow Mapping:** Prepare a detailed flow chart covering all wet-processing stages — desizing, scouring, bleaching, mercerizing, dyeing, printing, and finishing — listing chemical and water inputs at each step.

**Stage 3 — Data Collection:** Install or read flow meters at each process unit. Record chemical doses, fabric mass before/after, and operational parameters (temperature, time, batch size).

**Stage 4 — Sampling & Analysis:** Collect composite samples of each effluent stream. Analyse for **pH, COD, BOD, TSS, TDS, salinity, residual dye concentration, and ADMI colour units**.

**Stage 5 — Mass Balance Construction:** Apply $\text{Input} = \text{Output} + \text{Loss}$ to each unit operation. Quantify unfixed dye, salt losses, and water consumption per kg fabric.

**Stage 6 — Identify P2 Opportunities:** For every major loss identified, list possible source-reduction measures (e.g., enzymatic desizing, RFT dyeing, low-liquor-ratio machines).

**Stage 7 — Ranking:** Rank each P2 opportunity by (i) pollution reduction potential, (ii) capital cost, (iii) payback period, and (iv) ease of implementation.

**Stage 8 — Reporting:** Submit an executive report with quantitative findings, prioritized P2 action plan, and verification protocols.

> *(Diagrammatic flow chart from Section 4.1 should be reproduced by the student for full marks.)*

---

**(b) Discuss any FIVE pollution prevention opportunities applicable to the textile wet-processing industry. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (any five):**

1. **Right-First-Time (RFT) Dyeing:** Computerized dye and chemical dosing systems achieve correct shade in the first attempt, eliminating re-dyeing. Reduces dye and salt waste by **30–50 %**.

2. **Enzymatic Desizing & Bio-Scouring:** Replacing hot acidic/alkaline desizing with amylase enzymes, and alkaline scouring with pectinase enzymes. Operates at lower temperature, generates biodegradable effluent, and reduces **COD load by 40–60 %**.

3. **Low Liquor Ratio Dyeing Machines:** Modern jet or air-jet dyeing machines operate at liquor ratio 1:4 to 1:6 instead of conventional 1:10. Saves **up to 60 % water and 40 % energy**.

4. **Counter-Current Washing:** Fabric and water flow in opposite directions. The cleanest water contacts the freshest fabric. Reduces fresh water consumption by **50–70 %**.

5. **H₂O₂ Bleaching with Activators:** Replacing sodium hypochlorite with hydrogen peroxide (with TAED or NOBS activators) eliminates absorbable organochlorines (AOX), a regulated toxic effluent parameter.

6. **Caustic Recovery by Membrane Filtration:** From mercerizing liquors, **60–80 %** NaOH can be recovered using nanofiltration, reducing both chemical cost and effluent salinity.

7. **Digital Printing:** Eliminates screen preparation, reduces wash-water volume by **80 %**, and enables just-in-time production — minimizes over-production waste.

> Each measure must be justified with both an *environmental* benefit and an *economic* justification for full 7-mark credit.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) Construct a mass balance for a reactive dyeing process with the following data and identify the major pollution loads: Fabric = 100 kg; LR = 1:8; Dye applied = 5 kg; Fixation = 65 %; Salt applied = 50 kg; Salt lost to effluent = 40 kg; Effluent volume per batch = 1600 L. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Effluent Volume**

$$
V_{\text{eff}} = LR \times M_f \times 2 \text{ (dyebath + wash)} = 8 \times 100 \times 2 = 1600\ \text{L}
$$

**Step 2: Mass balance on Dye**

$$
D_{\text{fixed}} = 0.65 \times 5 = 3.25\ \text{kg}
$$

$$
D_{\text{effluent}} = 5 - 3.25 = 1.75\ \text{kg/batch}
$$

**Step 3: Dye concentration in effluent**

$$
C_{\text{dye}} = \dfrac{1.75\ \text{kg}}{1600\ \text{L}} = 1.094 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/L} = 1094\ \text{mg/L}
$$

**Step 4: Salt pollution load per batch**

$$
PL_{\text{salt}} = 40\ \text{kg/batch}
$$

**Step 5: Daily pollution load (2 batches/day)**

$$
Q_{\text{daily}} = 2 \times 1600 = 3200\ \text{L/day} = 3.2\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

$$
PL_{\text{dye,daily}} = 3.2 \times 1.094 = 3.50\ \text{kg/day}
$$

$$
PL_{\text{salt,daily}} = 2 \times 40 = 80\ \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 6: Specific Water Consumption**

$$
SWC = \dfrac{3200\ \text{L/day}}{200\ \text{kg/day}} = 16\ \text{L/kg}
$$

**Major Pollution Loads Identified:**
- **Dye (3.50 kg/day)** — colour and COD contributor.
- **Salt (80 kg/day)** — TDS and aquatic toxicity.
- **Water (3.2 m³/day)** — high specific consumption.

---

**(b) Suggest P2 measures to reduce each of the above pollution loads by at least 50 %. Justify each with an engineering principle. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Pollution Load | P2 Measure | Engineering Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **Dye loss (1.75 kg/batch)** | Use **high-fixation reactive dyes** (bifunctional, fixation ≥ 90 %) + **RFT dyeing** | Increased covalent bonding reduces unfixed dye fraction |
| **Salt loss (40 kg/batch)** | **Cationic pre-treatment of cotton** (e.g., with quaternary ammonium reagents) — eliminates need for electrolyte | Modifies fibre surface charge so dye-fibre attraction occurs without salt |
| **Water use (1600 L/batch)** | **Counter-current washing** + **low LR dyeing (1:5)** | Reverse flow gradient reduces freshwater demand; lower LR reduces total liquor mass |
| **Effluent colour** | **Membrane nanofiltration of dyebath** — recovers unfixed dye for reuse | Size-exclusion and Donnan exclusion retain dye molecules while passing water and salts |

**Quantitative Verification (after P2):**

- Dye effluent: $1.75 \rightarrow 0.40$ kg/batch → **77 % reduction** ✓
- Salt effluent: $40 \rightarrow 20$ kg/batch → **50 % reduction** ✓
- Water: $1600 \rightarrow 960$ L/batch → **40 % reduction** (combine with reuse → ≥ 50 %) ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "waste audit" with "waste management"** — A waste audit is a *diagnostic* exercise, not a treatment or disposal activity. Writing about ETP design in a waste-audit question will lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Skipping numerical units** — Always pair every numerical answer with units (kg/day, L/kg, mg/L). KTU examiners strictly enforce this.
> 3. **Listing P2 measures without engineering justification** — "Use enzymes for desizing" alone is worth only 1 mark. Adding *why* (lower temperature, biodegradable effluent, COD reduction %) earns full 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Wrong sequencing of audit steps** — Sampling before process mapping, or reporting before mass balance — this breaks the logical flow and attracts a "lack of coherence" penalty.
> 5. **Confusing Pollution Prevention (P2) with Recycling** — Recycling is one rank *below* P2 in the hierarchy. Writing "recycling is the best pollution prevention method" loses a hierarchy mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Waste Audits and Pollution Prevention in Textile:**

- **Definition recall:** Waste audit = systematic identification, quantification, and characterization of waste streams.
- **Audit steps:** Planning → Process mapping → Data collection → Sampling → Mass balance → P2 identification → Ranking → Reporting.
- **Hierarchy order (top to bottom):** Source Reduction → Reuse/Recycle → Treatment → Disposal.
- **Wet-processing stages:** Desizing → Scouring → Bleaching → Mercerizing → Dyeing → Printing → Finishing.
- **Biggest pollution contributors:** Dyeing (unfixed dye + salt) and Bleaching (AOX).
- **Key P2 measures to memorize:** Enzymatic desizing, bio-scouring, H₂O₂ bleaching, RFT dyeing, high-fixation reactive dyes, low-LR machines, counter-current wash, membrane caustic recovery, digital printing.
- **Quantitative benchmarks:** Conventional reactive dye fixation 60–70 %; achievable ≥ 90 %; conventional SWC ~100–150 L/kg; achievable ≤ 50 L/kg.
- **Formulas to retain:**
  - $PL = Q \times C$
  - $\eta_F = D_{\text{fixed}}/D_{\text{applied}} \times 100$
  - $SWC = V_{\text{water}}/M_{\text{fabric}}$
  - $\%R = (C_{\text{before}} - C_{\text{after}})/C_{\text{before}} \times 100$
  - $SPP = \text{CapEx}/NAB$
- **Exam pattern:** Part A — 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks; Part B — 1 question × 14 marks (with internal choice). Total module contribution = **20 marks**.
- **Always quantify P2 benefit** with a percentage reduction in mass balance to score full marks.
- **Connect every P2 measure** to either a chemical, engineering, or process-control principle for high-scoring answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sugar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT831 - ENVIRONMENT MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sugar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Sugar Industry (in Environmental Context):**
> The sugar industry is classified as one of the **most polluting and resource-intensive agro-based industries** under the **17 categories of highly polluting industries** identified by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), India. It converts sugarcane (*Saccharum officinarum*) into refined sugar through a sequence of crushing, juice extraction, clarification, evaporation, crystallization, and centrifugation stages, generating large volumes of **high-strength organic wastewater**, **solid waste** (bagasse, press mud, fly ash), and **air emissions** (particulates, SO₂, NOx, CO₂).

**KTU Syllabus Mapping (Module 3 – Industrial Pollution & Waste Management):**
The "Sugar" topic is examined as a **representative case study** of an agro-based polluting industry, covering its process flow, raw materials, products, by-products, and most importantly — its **environmental footprint** and **waste management protocols**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a sugar mill like a giant kitchen preparing jaggery at industrial scale.**
> When you crush sugarcane at home to extract juice, the leftover fibrous pulp (bagasse) is thrown away, the muddy sludge (press mud) settles at the bottom, steam rises from boiling vats, and the sweet juice evaporates into thick syrup. Now imagine doing this for **5,000 tonnes of cane per day** — the "kitchen waste" multiplies into tons of polluting effluents, mountains of bagasse, and clouds of smoke. The environmental manager's job is to **treat every drop of wastewater**, **recycle the bagasse as fuel**, and **capture particulates** before they escape the chimney.

---

### 1.3 Key Environmental Metrics & Constants

The following constants and standard parameters govern sugar mill environmental design:

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Cane crush capacity** | **2,500 – 5,000 TCD** (Tonnes Cane per Day) | Plant sizing |
| **Water consumption** | **10 – 25 m³ per tonne of cane** | Highest among food industries |
| **Wastewater generation** | **0.5 – 1.0 m³ per tonne of cane** | Direct effluent load |
| **Wastewater BOD₅** | **2,000 – 6,000 mg/L** | Extremely high organic load |
| **Wastewater COD** | **4,000 – 10,000 mg/L** | Indicates non-biodegradable fraction |
| **Wastewater pH** | **4.5 – 7.0** | Acidic to neutral |
| **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** | **1,500 – 4,000 mg/L** | High colloidal load |
| **Bagasse generation** | **~30 % of cane weight** | Solid waste / fuel |
| **Press mud generation** | **~3 – 5 % of cane weight** | Solid waste / fertilizer |
| **Molasses generation** | **~4 – 5 % of cane weight** | By-product / distillery feedstock |
| **Indian sugar industry size** | **~530 operating mills**, **~35 million tonnes** annual production | National footprint |
| **CPCB effluent discharge norm BOD₅** | **≤ 100 mg/L** (inland surface water) | Regulatory limit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold constants** above are mandatory recall values for KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sugar Manufacturing Process — Stepwise Logic

The sugar manufacturing process generates distinct **waste streams at each stage**. The KTU examiner expects students to map every unit operation with its corresponding waste type.

#### Stage 1: Cane Receiving & Preparation
- **Operation:** Unloading, weighing, washing of sugarcane.
- **Cane Wash Water** → contains soil, dirt, grit, organic debris.
- **Environmental concern:** High TSS, biodegradable but with high BOD.

#### Stage 2: Crushing / Milling
- **Operation:** Roller mills extract ~65–70 % juice from cane.
- **Bagasse** (fibrous residue, ~30 % of cane weight) is produced.
- **Imbibition water** is added to extract residual sugar → generates **mixed juice**.
- **Environmental concern:** Bagasse disposal; imbibition water increases wastewater volume.

#### Stage 3: Juice Clarification
- **Operation:** Phosphoric acid + lime added → heated → **calcium phosphate floc** carries impurities down as **press mud (filter cake)**.
- **Press mud** (~3–5 % of cane) is generated as **solid waste**.
- **Clarified juice** proceeds forward.

#### Stage 4: Evaporation
- **Operation:** Multi-effect evaporators concentrate juice from ~15 % to ~50–60 % solids → **thick juice / syrup**.
- **Waste:** **Condenser cooling water** (warm water, low pollution but thermally polluting).

#### Stage 5: Crystallization (Pan Boiling)
- **Operation:** Vacuum pans crystallize sugar → produces **massecuite**.

#### Stage 6: Centrifugation & Drying
- **Operation:** Centrifuges separate sugar crystals from **molasses**.
- **Molasses** (~4–5 % of cane) is the **by-product** — sent to distilleries for ethanol.

---

### 2.2 Waste Streams Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Point:** Sugar mill generates **three types of waste** that map to the three environmental media:

| Environmental Medium | Waste Stream | Origin | Treatment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Water Pollution** | Spent wash, condensates, cane wash, floor washings, boiler blowdown | Entire process | ETP — Anaerobic + Aerobic |
| **Air Pollution** | Particulates (PM), SO₂, NOx, CO, fly ash | Boilers burning bagasse | ESP / Bag filter / Wet scrubber |
| **Solid Waste** | Bagasse (used as fuel), press mud (used as manure), ETP sludge | Crushing, clarification, ETP | Composting, landfill |

---

### 2.3 Detailed Environmental Impacts

#### A. Water Pollution
- **Sugar mill effluent** is characterized by high **BOD, COD, suspended solids, color, and odor**.
- The high BOD is due to dissolved sugars, organic acids, and suspended cane particles.
- **Color** arises from melanoidin pigments (Maillard reaction products).
- Discharged without treatment → depletes dissolved oxygen → kills aquatic life (eutrophication).

#### B. Air Pollution
- **Bagasse-fired boilers** emit **particulate matter (PM)** due to incomplete combustion.
- **SO₂ emissions** arise from sulfur in bagasse.
- **NOx** is formed at high combustion temperatures.
- **Fly ash** is a solid waste from boilers.

#### C. Land Pollution
- **Press mud and ETP sludge** contain organic matter and trace heavy metals.
- Indiscriminate dumping contaminates soil and groundwater.

#### D. Noise Pollution
- Crushing mills, centrifuges, and boilers generate high noise levels (>85 dB).

---

### 2.4 Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP) for Sugar Industry

The standard ETP follows a **combined anaerobic-aerobic biological treatment** train:

```
Raw Effluent Collection Tank
        ↓
  Screening & Grit Removal
        ↓
  Oil & Grease Removal
        ↓
Equalization Tank (with pH correction)
        ↓
Primary Clarifier
        ↓
Anaerobic Digester (UASB / Anaerobic Filter)
        ↓
Aerobic Biological Treatment (ASP / Activated Sludge / Trickling Filter)
        ↓
Secondary Clarifier
        ↓
Tertiary Treatment (Polishing — Sand filter / Activated carbon)
        ↓
Disinfection (Chlorination / UV)
        ↓
Sludge Treatment (Thickener → Digester → Drying Beds)
        ↓
Treated Effluent to Discharge / Reuse
```

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are direct KTU exam-relevant derivations.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $BOD_5 = \dfrac{DO_{initial} - DO_{final}}{Volume\ of\ sample}$ | Standard 5-day BOD at **20 °C** | mg/L |
| 2 | $COD = \dfrac{(V_{blank} - V_{sample}) \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{V_{sample}}$ | Dichromate closed-reflux COD | mg/L |
| 3 | $BOD\ removal\ efficiency\ (\%) = \dfrac{BOD_{in} - BOD_{out}}{BOD_{in}} \times 100$ | ETP performance indicator | % |
| 4 | $Total\ wastewater\ (m^3/day) = Cane\ crush\ (TCD) \times 0.5\ to\ 1.0$ | Effluent volume estimation | m³/day |
| 5 | $Total\ BOD\ load\ (kg/day) = Flow \times BOD_{in}$ | Organic load to ETP | kg/day |
| 6 | $F/M\ ratio = \dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{V \times X}$ | Food-to-Microorganism ratio (ASP design) | day⁻¹ |
| 7 | $Hydraulic\ Retention\ Time\ (HRT) = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Reactor sizing | hours |
| 8 | $Volumetric\ Organic\ Loading\ (VLR) = \dfrac{Q \times BOD_{in}}{V}$ | UASB design parameter | kg BOD/m³·day |
| 9 | $Oxygen\ Required\ (kg/day) = a \times Q \times BOD_{removed} + b \times V \times X$ | Aeration tank design | kg O₂/day |
| 10 | $Specific\ Oxygen\ Uptake\ Rate\ (SOUR) = \dfrac{OUR}{MLVSS}$ | Sludge health indicator | mg O₂/g VSS·h |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above design parameters are used by **environmental consultants** and **ETP contractors** to design sugar mill effluent treatment plants across India. For example, a **2,500 TCD sugar mill** producing **2,000 m³/day of wastewater** at **4,000 mg/L BOD** has a **daily BOD load of 8,000 kg/day** — equivalent to the sewage load of a city of **~1.5 lakh population**. Treating this requires a **UASB reactor of ~1,600 m³** (at VLR = 5 kg/m³·day) followed by a **trickling filter or ASP** for polishing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example — Sugar Mill ETP Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU-style):**
> A sugar mill crushes **3,000 tonnes of cane per day (TCD)**. Wastewater generation is **0.8 m³ per tonne of cane**. The **influent BOD₅ is 4,500 mg/L**. The effluent discharge standard mandates **BOD₅ ≤ 100 mg/L** for inland surface water discharge. The ETP uses a **UASB reactor** followed by an **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)**.
>
> **Given:**
> - UASB Volumetric Loading Rate (VLR) = **5 kg BOD/m³·day**
> - UASB BOD removal efficiency = **70 %**
> - ASP F/M ratio = **0.25 day⁻¹**
> - ASP MLVSS concentration (X) = **3,000 mg/L**
> - ASP BOD removal efficiency = **85 %** (of incoming BOD after UASB)
>
> **Calculate:**
> 1. Daily wastewater flow (m³/day)
> 2. Daily BOD load to ETP (kg/day)
> 3. Volume of UASB reactor (m³)
> 4. BOD after UASB (mg/L)
> 5. BOD after ASP — verify against discharge norm
> 6. Volume of aeration tank (m³)

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

#### **Step 1 — Daily Wastewater Flow (Q)**

We are given that wastewater generation is **0.8 m³ per tonne of cane** and the mill crushes **3,000 TCD**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= Cane\ crush\ (TCD) \times Wastewater\ per\ tonne \\
  &= 3000 \times 0.8 \\
  &= 2400\ m^3/day
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A 3,000 TCD sugar mill produces **2,400 m³/day of wastewater** — equivalent to the daily sewage of a town with ~24,000 residents.

---

#### **Step 2 — Daily BOD Load to ETP**

The total organic load entering the treatment system is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
BOD\ load &= Q \times BOD_{in} \\
          &= 2400\ m^3/day \times 4500\ mg/L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Convert units:** $\dfrac{4500\ mg}{L} = \dfrac{4500\ g}{m^3} = \dfrac{4.5\ kg}{m^3}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
BOD\ load &= 2400 \times 4.5 \\
          &= 10800\ kg\ BOD/day
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The mill delivers **10,800 kg of organic load daily** — the biological equivalent of a **2-lakh-population city**.

---

#### **Step 3 — Volume of UASB Reactor**

Using the design parameter **VLR = 5 kg BOD/m³·day**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{UASB} &= \dfrac{BOD\ load}{VLR} \\
         &= \dfrac{10800\ kg/day}{5\ kg/m^3/day} \\
         &= 2160\ m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The UASB reactor requires a volume of **2,160 m³** (e.g., a circular tank of ~16 m diameter and ~11 m depth).

---

#### **Step 4 — BOD After UASB (Effluent of Anaerobic Stage)**

UASB achieves **70 % BOD removal**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
BOD_{after\ UASB} &= BOD_{in} \times (1 - 0.70) \\
                  &= 4500 \times 0.30 \\
                  &= 1350\ mg/L
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Anaerobic digestion reduces BOD from **4,500 → 1,350 mg/L**, but this is still **13.5× above the discharge norm** of 100 mg/L. Aerobic polishing is mandatory.

---

#### **Step 5 — BOD After ASP**

ASP removes **85 %** of the incoming BOD (post-UASB):

$$
\begin{aligned}
BOD_{after\ ASP} &= BOD_{after\ UASB} \times (1 - 0.85) \\
                 &= 1350 \times 0.15 \\
                 &= 202.5\ mg/L
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The discharge BOD is 202.5 mg/L — STILL ABOVE the 100 mg/L CPCB norm!**
> **Mitigation:** Increase ASP MLVSS, reduce F/M ratio, or add **tertiary polishing (sand filter + activated carbon)** to bring BOD below 100 mg/L.

---

#### **Step 6 — Volume of Aeration Tank (Using F/M Ratio)**

The F/M formula is:

$$
F/M = \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{V \times X}
$$

Rearranging for V:

$$
V_{ASP} = \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{F/M \times X}
$$

**Unit conversion:** $BOD_{in}\ to\ ASP = 1350\ mg/L = 1.35\ kg/m^3$; $X = 3000\ mg/L = 3.0\ kg/m^3$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ASP} &= \dfrac{2400\ m^3/day \times 1.35\ kg/m^3}{0.25\ day^{-1} \times 3.0\ kg/m^3} \\
        &= \dfrac{3240}{0.75} \\
        &= 4320\ m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The aeration tank requires **4,320 m³** — a large concrete structure, typically rectangular, with diffused aeration.

---

#### **Step 7 — Summary Table**

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Daily flow (Q) | **2,400** | m³/day |
| BOD load | **10,800** | kg/day |
| UASB volume | **2,160** | m³ |
| BOD after UASB | **1,350** | mg/L |
| BOD after ASP | **202.5** | mg/L (non-compliant) |
| ASP aeration volume | **4,320** | m³ |

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Industry Comparison (KTU High-Yield)

| Industry | Wastewater (m³/T) | BOD (mg/L) | Treatment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sugar** | **0.5 – 1.0** | **2,000 – 6,000** | UASB + ASP |
| Distillery | 12 – 15 | 40,000 – 60,000 | Biomethanation + ASP |
| Pulp & Paper | 200 – 250 | 1,000 – 2,000 | Primary + Biological + Tertiary |
| Tannery | 30 – 35 | 2,000 – 4,000 | Physico-chemical + Biological |
| Dairy | 3 – 5 | 1,000 – 2,500 | Aerobic biological |

> [!NOTE]
> Sugar ranks as the **second most water-polluting** agro-based industry after **distilleries** (which consume sugar mill molasses as feedstock).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

    P7 --> P8

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1: List the major waste streams generated by a sugar industry and their environmental significance.**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The sugar industry generates **three major categories of waste streams**:

1. **Liquid Waste — Wastewater / Spent Wash:** Generated from cane washing, imbibition, juice clarification, condensates, and floor washings. Characterized by **high BOD (2,000–6,000 mg/L), high COD (4,000–10,000 mg/L), acidic pH (4.5–7.0), high TSS, and dark color** due to melanoidin pigments. Causes **eutrophication** of receiving water bodies.

2. **Solid Waste — Bagasse, Press Mud, ETP Sludge, Fly Ash:** Bagasse (~30 % of cane) is reused as boiler fuel; press mud (~3–5 %) is used as manure/compost; fly ash from boilers is disposed in ash ponds.

3. **Air Emissions — Particulates, SO₂, NOx, CO:** Released from **bagasse-fired boilers** due to incomplete combustion and sulfur content of bagasse. Causes **respiratory ailments, smog, and acid rain**.

*(1 mark per type — 3 marks total)*

---

#### **Question 2: What is the role of a UASB reactor in a sugar mill ETP?**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB)** reactor is the **primary biological treatment unit** in a sugar mill ETP. Its roles are:

1. **High organic load reduction:** Treats high-strength wastewater with **BOD removal efficiency of 60–80 %** at organic loading rates of **5–15 kg BOD/m³·day**.

2. **Biogas generation:** Converts organic pollutants into **methane-rich biogas** (60–70 % CH₄), which is a **renewable energy source** used to fire boilers, reducing fossil fuel consumption.

3. **Sludge blanket formation:** Biomass granulates into dense sludge at the bottom, providing high solids retention even at high hydraulic loads.

*(1 mark per point — 3 marks total)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Describe the **manufacturing process of sugar** with a neat flow diagram, highlighting the **environmental waste streams** generated at each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **characteristics of sugar mill wastewater** in detail. Design a **complete Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)** flow scheme suitable for a **2,500 TCD sugar mill**, justifying each unit operation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sugar Manufacturing Process & Waste Streams (7 Marks):**

| Stage | Unit Operation | Waste Generated | Environmental Concern |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cane receiving, washing | Cane wash water | High TSS, soil |
| 2 | Crushing (roller mills) | Bagasse, imbibition water | Solid waste, increased effluent volume |
| 3 | Clarification (lime + H₃PO₄) | Press mud (filter cake) | Solid waste (rich in organic) |
| 4 | Evaporation (multi-effect) | Condenser cooling water | Thermal pollution |
| 5 | Crystallization (pan boiling) | Minimal waste | — |
| 6 | Centrifugation | Molasses (by-product) | Used for ethanol |
| 7 | Boiler (bagasse-fired) | Fly ash, PM, SO₂, NOx | Air pollution |

**Flow Diagram** *(Refer Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram)*. **(3 Marks for diagram + 4 Marks for explanation)**

---

**(b) Wastewater Characteristics & ETP Design (7 Marks):**

**Characteristics of sugar mill wastewater:**

- **Flow:** 0.5–1.0 m³ per tonne of cane crushed
- **BOD₅:** 2,000–6,000 mg/L
- **COD:** 4,000–10,000 mg/L
- **pH:** 4.5–7.0 (slightly acidic due to organic acids)
- **TSS:** 1,500–4,000 mg/L
- **Color:** Dark brown (melanoidins)
- **Odour:** Foul, due to sulfides and organic acids

**ETP Design for 2,500 TCD mill (with detailed justification):**

| Unit Operation | Justification |
|---|---|
| **Collection tank** | Buffers shock loads |
| **Screening & grit removal** | Removes floating debris and heavy solids |
| **Oil & grease trap** | Prevents scum in biological units |
| **Equalization tank** | Homogenizes flow and quality |
| **Primary clarifier** | Removes 60–70 % TSS by gravity settling |
| **UASB reactor** | High-rate anaerobic digestion; biogas recovery |
| **Aeration tank (ASP)** | Polishes remaining BOD; nitrification |
| **Secondary clarifier** | Solid-liquid separation of biomass |
| **Tertiary filtration** | Polishing to meet <100 mg/L BOD |
| **Disinfection** | Pathogen kill |
| **Sludge thickener & drying beds** | Solid waste management |

**(3 Marks for characteristics + 4 Marks for ETP design)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** What are the **major air pollutants emitted by a sugar mill**? Explain the **air pollution control measures (APCM)** adopted in bagasse-fired boilers with a neat sketch. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **volume of the UASB reactor** and **aeration tank** for a sugar mill with the following data. Verify whether the treated effluent meets the **CPCB discharge norm of BOD ≤ 100 mg/L**. **(7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- Cane crush = **4,000 TCD**
- Wastewater generation = **0.7 m³/tonne of cane**
- Influent BOD = **5,000 mg/L**
- UASB VLR = **6 kg BOD/m³·day**, UASB removal = **75 %**
- ASP F/M = **0.30 day⁻¹**, MLVSS = **3,500 mg/L**, ASP removal = **80 %**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Air Pollutants & APCM in Sugar Mills (7 Marks):**

**Major air pollutants from bagasse-fired boilers:**
1. **Particulate Matter (PM)** — from incomplete combustion; major contributor.
2. **Sulfur Dioxide (SO₂)** — from sulfur in bagasse (~0.1 % S).
3. **Nitrogen Oxides (NOx)** — high-temperature combustion of bagasse.
4. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** — incomplete combustion.
5. **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)** — from moisture in bagasse.

**Air Pollution Control Measures (APCM):**

| Control Device | Working Principle | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|
| **Cyclone separator** | Centrifugal separation | 70–90 % for PM |
| **Wet scrubber** | Gas-liquid contact | 90–95 % for PM and SO₂ |
| **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** | Charged particles collected on plates | >99 % for PM |
| **Baghouse (fabric filter)** | Mechanical filtration | >99.9 % for PM |
| **Flue Gas Desulfurization (FGD)** | Lime/limestone slurry absorbs SO₂ | 90–95 % SO₂ removal |

**Most commonly used:** **Cyclone + Wet Scrubber combination** for sugar mills in India. **(4 Marks for APCM + 3 Marks for pollutants)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Daily wastewater flow:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 4000 \times 0.7 = 2800\ m^3/day
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily BOD load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BOD\ load &= 2800 \times \dfrac{5000}{1000} = 2800 \times 5 = 14000\ kg/day
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — UASB volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{UASB} &= \dfrac{14000}{6} = 2333.33\ m^3 \approx 2334\ m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — BOD after UASB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BOD_{out,UASB} &= 5000 \times (1 - 0.75) = 1250\ mg/L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — BOD after ASP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BOD_{out,ASP} &= 1250 \times (1 - 0.80) = 250\ mg/L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Aeration tank volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ASP} &= \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{F/M \times X} \\
        &= \dfrac{2800 \times 1.25}{0.30 \times 3.5} \\
        &= \dfrac{3500}{1.05} = 3333.33\ m^3 \approx 3334\ m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification against CPCB norm:**

| Parameter | Calculated | Norm | Compliant? |
|---|---|---|---|
| BOD after ASP | **250 mg/L** | **≤ 100 mg/L** | **❌ NON-COMPLIANT** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Students commonly forget to convert mg/L to kg/m³. **1 mg/L = 1 g/m³ = 0.001 kg/m³.** Always write `S₀ (in kg/m³) = BOD (in mg/L) / 1000`.
> 2. **Effluent norm check:** Many students stop after calculating BOD without verifying against **CPCB standards**. This is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Mitigation:** The 250 mg/L non-compliance requires **tertiary treatment** — add **sand filtration + activated carbon + chlorination** to bring BOD below 100 mg/L.
> 4. **F/M ratio sign convention:** Some students invert the formula. Correct: $F/M = \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{V \times X}$, where F = food supplied (kg/day) and M = microbial mass (kg).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Sugar Industry"**

- ✅ Sugar industry is a **17-category highly polluting industry** (CPCB list).
- ✅ Cane crushing capacity is measured in **TCD (Tonnes Cane per Day)**.
- ✅ Wastewater generation = **0.5 – 1.0 m³ per tonne of cane**.
- ✅ Sugar mill wastewater BOD = **2,000 – 6,000 mg/L**; COD = **4,000 – 10,000 mg/L**.
- ✅ pH of effluent = **4.5 – 7.0** (acidic).
- ✅ Bagasse = **~30 % of cane weight** (used as boiler fuel).
- ✅ Press mud = **~3 – 5 % of cane weight** (used as manure).
- ✅ Molasses = **~4 – 5 % of cane weight** (by-product → distillery).
- ✅ Standard ETP = **UASB + ASP + Tertiary + Disinfection**.
- ✅ UASB removes **70–80 % BOD**; ASP removes additional **80–85 %**.
- ✅ **CPCB discharge norm:** BOD ≤ **100 mg/L** for inland surface water; TSS ≤ **100 mg/L**.
- ✅ APCM for bagasse boilers: **Cyclone + Wet Scrubber** or **ESP**.
- ✅ Melanoidin pigments cause **dark brown color** in effluent.
- ✅ Sugar mill effluent is **anaerobically biodegradable** — hence UASB is preferred.
- ✅ Aerobic treatment uses **F/M ratio of 0.2–0.5 day⁻¹** with **MLVSS 2,000–4,000 mg/L**.
- ✅ Biogas from UASB is **60–70 % methane** — renewable energy recovery.
- ✅ Always **verify treated effluent quality** against CPCB norms in numerical problems.
- ✅ Common exam pitfall: **unit conversion (mg/L → kg/m³)** — divide by 1,000.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulp & Paper
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT831 - ENVIRONMENT MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pulp & Paper

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Pulp and Paper Industry: A Definition

The **Pulp and Paper Industry** is a resource-intensive manufacturing sector that converts lignocellulosic raw materials (wood, agricultural residues, recycled fibres) into pulp, which is subsequently processed into paper, paperboard, and related cellulose-based products. From an **Environmental Management Systems (EMS)** perspective, the industry is classified as one of the **most polluting heavy-process industries** in the world, generating significant emissions to air, water, and land at every stage of the value chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 Focus):**
> The course OECHT831 examines *Pulp & Paper* specifically through the lens of **environmental burden**, **process-integrated pollution control**, **ISO 14001 EMS implementation**, and **cleaner production strategies**. Numerical design calculations are NOT the focus — process understanding, pollution inventory, and control technologies are.

## 1.2 Industry Segments & Products

| Segment | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Wood pulp** | Virgin fibre from softwood/hardwood | Bleached / unbleached kraft pulp |
| **Recovered paper** | Recycled fibre processing | OCC, mixed waste paper |
| **Printing & writing** | Graphic papers | A4, copier paper |
| **Packaging** | Containerboard, cartons | Kraft liner, corrugated boxes |
| **Tissue & sanitary** | Absorbent hygiene products | Toilet tissue, napkins |
| **Specialty papers** | High-value engineered paper | Filter paper, currency, electrical insulation |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tree-to-Toilet-Paper" Journey

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Think of pulp manufacture as **cooking a tree in a giant chemical pressure cooker** to separate the useful cellulose fibres from the unwanted glue-like substance (lignin). Once the fibres are liberated, washed, and bleached white, they are spread thin on a fast-moving wire mesh, dewatered, pressed, dried, and rolled — much like **drying a thin sheet of noodle dough on a conveyor belt**. The industry is the *only* major manufacturing process that uses trees as its primary feedstock, which is why its environmental footprint is uniquely tied to **deforestation, water use, and chlorinated organics**.

## 1.4 Why This Industry Matters in Environmental Management

- **Water intensity:** Typically **10–60 m³ of water per tonne of paper** produced (highly process-dependent).
- **Energy intensity:** Pulp and paper ranks among the **top 5 most energy-intensive industries** globally.
- **Air emissions:** Sulfur compounds (TRS — Total Reduced Sulfur), NO_x, CO, CO₂, particulate matter, and historically **chlorinated dioxins/furans**.
- **Solid waste:** Sludge, bark, ash, lime grits, and biological treatment residuals.
- **Forest stewardship:** The industry is the single largest industrial user of wood fibre on Earth.

## 1.5 Core Environmental Metrics (Industry Benchmarks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industry Reference Values (per tonne of air-dried pulp — ADP):**
> - Water consumption: **30–60 m³/t**
> - Effluent volume: **40–80 m³/t**
> - COD load: **30–70 kg O₂/t**
> - BOD load: **15–35 kg O₂/t**
> - AOX (Adsorbable Organic Halides): **<0.25 kg/t** (modern ECF)
> - Energy: **18–25 GJ/t** (integrated mill)

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 Process Flow of an Integrated Pulp & Paper Mill

The integrated mill has **four major process blocks**, each with distinct environmental signatures:

### Block 1 — Fibre Preparation (Wood Handling)
1. Debarking of logs (mechanical abrasion / hydraulic).
2. Chipping into uniform wood chips (typically **15–25 mm** length).
3. Screening of oversized chips, fines, and knots.

> **Environmental relevance:** Bark and screening rejects become **solid waste (bark ash, biomaterial)**; debarking water carries dissolved organics.

### Block 2 — Pulping (Fibre Liberation)

Pulping is classified by the **mechanical vs. chemical** energy used to separate cellulose from lignin.

| Pulping Method | Chemical Used | Lignin Removed | Yield (%) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stone Groundwood (SGW)** | None (mechanical) | ~0% | 90–95 | Newsprint |
| **Thermomechanical (TMP)** | Steam + refining | ~0% | 85–92 | Magazine paper |
| **Chemithermomechanical (CTMP)** | Mild Na₂SO₃ | ~10–15% | 80–88 | Printing paper |
| **Sulfite Process** | HSO₃⁻ / SO₃²⁻ | Major | 45–55 | Dissolving pulp |
| **Kraft (Soda) Process** | NaOH + Na₂S | Major (≈90–95%) | 45–55 | Strong packaging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:**
> The **Kraft (Sulfate) process** is the **dominant global pulping technology (>70% of chemical pulp)** because it produces the strongest fibres, tolerates all wood species, and has an efficient chemical recovery cycle.

**The Kraft Cooking Reaction (Simplified):**

$$ \text{Lignin} + \text{NaOH} + \text{Na}_2\text{S} \xrightarrow{\Delta,\, 160\text{–}170\,°C} \text{Dissolved Lignin (Black Liquor)} + \text{Cellulose Fibres} $$

The dissolved lignin in alkaline solution is termed **"Black Liquor"** — a thick, dark, organic-rich spent liquor that is the **single most important waste/recovery stream** in the mill.

### Block 3 — Bleaching

Bleaching is required to whiten the brown kraft pulp. The classic sequence was **CEHH** (Chlorine → Extraction → Hypochlorite → Hypochlorite), but due to **dioxin formation**, modern mills use:

- **ECF** — Elemental Chlorine Free: e.g., **D₀(EP)D₁(EP)D₂** sequences
- **TCF** — Totally Chlorine Free: e.g., **OQP** (Oxygen + Peroxide) — used for premium markets

> **Key reagents:** ClO₂ (chlorine dioxide), H₂O₂, O₂, NaOH, ozone (O₃), peracids.

### Block 4 — Paper Making (Stock Preparation & Wet End)

1. Pulp is **refined** (beaten) to develop fibre bonding.
2. **Stock** is diluted to ~1% consistency with white water recirculation.
3. Additives added: **fillers (CaCO₃), sizing agents (AKD/rosin), optical brighteners, retention aids**.
4. Headbox → Fourdrinier wire (or twin-wire former) → Press section → Dryer section (steam-heated cylinders) → Calender → Reel.

## 2.2 KTU Concept Sheet — Environmental Burden by Process Stage

| Stage | Air Emissions | Water Pollutants | Solid/Hazardous Waste |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wood handling | Dust, VOCs (terpenes) | Suspended solids, tannins | Bark, wood waste |
| Pulping | TRS (H₂S, CH₃SH, DMS, DMDS), SO₂, Na fumes | Black liquor (high BOD, COD, colour) | Spent liquor, grits |
| Bleaching | Cl₂, ClO₂ leaks | AOX, chloroform, dioxins | Sludge |
| Recovery boiler | TRS, NO_x, SO₂, particulate | — | Smelt, ash |
| Paper machine | Steam VOCs | White water solids, stickies | Reject pulp, sludge |
| Effluent treatment | — | Treated effluent to river | Biosludge, chemical sludge |

## 2.3 The Kraft Chemical Recovery Cycle (KCU)

The Kraft process is economically and environmentally viable ONLY because of an integrated **chemical recovery loop**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Black Liquor → Evaporation (Multi-Effect Evaporator) → Recovery Boiler (combustion at ~1000°C) → Smelt Dissolution → Causticizing → Lime Kiln → White Liquor back to digester.**

**Recovery Boiler Functions:**
1. **Generates steam** (recovers chemical energy of organics in black liquor).
2. **Reduces Na₂SO₄ → Na₂S** (regenerates active cooking chemical).
3. **Converts inorganic salts to smelt** (Na₂CO₃ + Na₂S).

**Causticizing Reaction:**

$$ \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{NaOH} + \text{CaCO}_3 \quad (\text{CaCO}_3 \text{ is calcined back to CaO}) $$

This is a textbook example of **industrial symbiosis / circular economy** and is heavily tested in KTU EMS papers.

## 2.4 Why Pollutants Are Generated — The "Why" Behind the Burden

- **Lignin derivatives** in black liquor cause high **COD, BOD, and colour** (lignin chromophores absorb visible light).
- **Chlorine bleaching** generates **AOX** and **dioxins** through electrophilic substitution on residual lignin.
- **TRS emissions** arise from incomplete combustion or black liquor leaks — they are the industry’s signature odor complaint.
- **High water use** in washing stages (post-digester and post-bleaching) drives wastewater volume.
- **Adsorbable Organic Halides (AOX)** are the **regulated indicator parameter** for chlorinated organics in pulp mill effluent.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Application

The Kraft recovery cycle reduces external fuel demand by up to **50%** of total mill energy. Mills that adopt **biomass-based combined heat and power (CHP)** can become **net exporters of electricity**. This aligns directly with the **ISO 50001 (Energy Management)** and **ISO 14001 (Environmental Management)** frameworks that form the backbone of KTU Module 3.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Process Analysis & Environmental Calculations

## 3.1 Stoichiometric Walk-Through: Kraft Digestion

The active cooking species in Kraft pulping is the **hydrosulfide ion (HS⁻)** and **hydroxide ion (OH⁻)**. The overall digestion chemistry for a softwood can be approximated as:

**Step 1 — Lignin dissolution (illustrative mass balance):**

$$ \text{C}_{9}\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_{3}\,\text{(lignin unit)} + \text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{Na-lignin salts (in black liquor)} + \text{by-products} $$

**Step 2 — Carbohydrate protection:**
The HS⁻ ions preferentially attack lignin, **preserving cellulose** — this is why Kraft pulp is strong.

**Step 3 — H-factor (the practical digestion control parameter):**

The H-factor combines time and temperature into a single control variable:

$$ H = \int_{0}^{t_{\text{final}}} \exp\!\left(43.2 - \frac{16{,}113}{T(t)}\right) dt $$

where:

- $T(t)$ = cooking temperature in **Kelvin**
- $t$ = time in **hours**
- The exponential term is the relative reaction rate constant
- $H$ is dimensionless and cumulative

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Meaning of H-factor:**
> A higher H-factor ⇒ more delignification ⇒ lower yield, lower Kappa number (residual lignin), but weaker fibres. A modern Kraft cook targets **H-factor ≈ 1000–2000** and a **Kappa number 25–35**.

## 3.2 Kappa Number — The Bleachability Indicator

The **Kappa number (K)** measures residual lignin in pulp and is the primary control variable for downstream bleaching.

$$ K = \frac{V_{\text{KMnO}_4} \times N}{m_{\text{pulp, dry}}} $$

where:

- $V_{\text{KMnO}_4}$ = volume of **0.1 N KMnO₄** consumed (mL)
- $N$ = normality of the permanganate solution
- $m_{\text{pulp, dry}}$ = oven-dry mass of pulp (g)

> **Interpretation:** Higher K → more residual lignin → more bleaching chemicals required → higher environmental burden.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Black Liquor Evaporation Load

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Style Walk-Through (typical KTU Module 3 numerical type):**

**Given:** A Kraft mill produces **500 tonnes of air-dried pulp (ADP) per day**. The black liquor solids loading is **1.5 tonnes of dry solids per tonne of pulp** with an initial moisture content of **80%** (i.e., 20% solids by mass). The evaporator train must concentrate it to **65% solids** before the recovery boiler.

**Step A — Total black liquor mass generated per day:**

$$ m_{\text{BL, total}} = 500 \;\tfrac{\text{t pulp}}{\text{day}} \times 1.5 \;\tfrac{\text{t dry solids}}{\text{t pulp}} \times \frac{1}{0.20} = 3750 \;\text{t/day} $$

**Step B — Dry solids mass in the stream (conserved):**

$$ m_{\text{dry}} = 3750 \;\tfrac{\text{t}}{\text{day}} \times 0.20 = 750 \;\text{t/day} $$

**Step C — Mass of concentrated black liquor at 65% solids leaving evaporator:**

$$ m_{\text{conc}} = \frac{750}{0.65} = 1153.85 \;\text{t/day} $$

**Step D — Water evaporated per day (the evaporator load):**

$$ m_{\text{evap}} = 3750 - 1153.85 = 2596.15 \;\text{t water/day} $$

**Step E — Steam economy check (multi-effect evaporator, sextuple effect ≈ 5 kg water/kg steam):**

$$ \text{Steam required} = \frac{2596.15}{5} = 519.23 \;\text{t steam/day} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The evaporator train removes nearly **2,600 tonnes of water per day** — illustrating why the recovery boiler’s heat integration is critical to overall mill energy efficiency.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Effluent COD Load Calculation

**Given:** A paper mill discharges **45 m³ of effluent per tonne of pulp** with an **average COD of 850 mg/L**. What is the daily COD load for a **300 t/day** mill?

**Step 1 — Total effluent flow per day:**

$$ Q = 300 \;\tfrac{\text{t}}{\text{day}} \times 45 \;\tfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{t}} = 13{,}500 \;\text{m}^3/\text{day} $$

**Step 2 — Daily COD load:**

$$ \text{COD}_{\text{load}} = 13{,}500 \;\tfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{day}} \times 850 \;\tfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} \times \frac{1000\;\text{L}}{1\;\text{m}^3} \times \frac{1\;\text{kg}}{10^6\;\text{mg}} $$

$$ \text{COD}_{\text{load}} = 11{,}475 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{day} \;\approx\; 11.48 \;\text{t O}_2/\text{day} $$

**Step 3 — Per-tonne specific load:**

$$ \text{Specific COD} = \frac{11{,}475\;\text{kg/day}}{300\;\text{t/day}} = 38.25 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{t pulp} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation:** A modern well-run mill should target **<30 kg COD/t pulp**; values above 40 kg/t typically indicate opportunities for **in-process modification, brown-stock washing efficiency improvements, and condensate reuse**.

## 3.5 Bleach Plant Chemical Demand Estimation

**Given:** A ECF bleaching sequence D₀(EP)D₁ is applied to pulp of **Kappa number 30**. The rule-of-thumb ClO₂ demand is **2.5 × Kappa (as % on oven-dry pulp)**.

**Step 1 — Active chlorine equivalent required:**

$$ \text{ClO}_2 \text{ demand} = 2.5 \times 30 = 75\% \text{ on oven-dry pulp (OD)} $$

**Step 2 — Per-tonne chemical mass:**

$$ m_{\text{ClO}_2} = 0.75 \times 1000\;\text{kg pulp} = 750\;\text{kg ClO}_2/\text{t pulp} $$

> **Engineering note:** ClO₂ must be generated on-site (NaClO₃ + HCl + reducing agent) due to its explosive instability. The ClO₂ generator effluent (acidic chlorate) requires **dedicated neutralization** — a typical **EMS operational control (OCP) point** under ISO 14001.

## 4.4 Spinning Cone of Environmental Control (Sequential Priority)

| Priority Level | Strategy | Example in Pulp & Paper |
|---|---|---|
| **1 — Prevention at source** | Process modification | Extended cooking, low-Kappa pulping, O₂ delignification |
| **2 — Internal reuse** | Recirculation | White water reuse, heat recovery from hot effluent |
| **3 — External recycling** | Off-site recovery | Spent acid → on-site ClO₂ generation; sludge to farmland (regulated) |
| **4 — Treatment** | End-of-pipe | Biological effluent treatment, scrubbers for TRS |
| **5 — Disposal** | Final release | Treated discharge, secure landfill |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. Define the term "Kraft Process" and state any two environmental concerns associated with it.

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, OECHT831, Module 3]`

**Model Answer:**
The **Kraft (Sulfate) Process** is a chemical pulping method in which wood chips are cooked in a solution of **sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and sodium sulfide (Na₂S)** — termed "white liquor" — at temperatures of **160–170 °C** to dissolve lignin and liberate cellulose fibres.

**Two environmental concerns:**
1. **Total Reduced Sulfur (TRS) emissions** — H₂S, methyl mercaptan, DMS, and DMDS released from digesters and recovery boilers cause severe odor and air-quality problems.
2. **High-strength black liquor** — contains dissolved lignin and contributes massive **COD, BOD, colour, and toxicity** to water bodies if not recovered.

**CO Mapping:** CO2 (Apply) | **Bloom's Level:** L3

---

### Q2. What is meant by ECF bleaching? How does it differ from conventional chlorine bleaching?

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OECHT831, Module 3]`

**Model Answer:**
**ECF (Elemental Chlorine Free)** bleaching is a pulp-bleaching process that **replaces molecular chlorine (Cl₂)** with **chlorine dioxide (ClO₂)** as the primary oxidant.

**Key differences from conventional Cl₂ bleaching:**

| Parameter | Conventional (CEHH) | ECF (D₀(EP)D₁D₂) |
|---|---|---|
| Primary oxidant | Cl₂ | ClO₂ |
| AOX generation | High | Reduced by ~70% |
| Dioxin/furan formation | Detectable | Below detection limit |
| Pulp strength | Lower | Better preserved |
| Bleach filtrate toxicity | High | Significantly lower |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** ECF does NOT mean "chlorine-free." It means no *elemental* chlorine. Students who write "ECF = no chlorine" will lose 1 mark. Use the exact phrase **"no molecular chlorine"**.

**CO Mapping:** CO2 | **Bloom's Level:** L2 (Understand)

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Full ESE Module Choice)

### Question A — Integrated Kraft Mill & Environmental Management

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, OECHT831, Module 3, Full 14 Marks]`

**Q. (a)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the **process flow of an integrated Kraft pulp and paper mill** highlighting the major process units. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** Explain the **principle of chemical recovery in the Kraft process**. Why is the recovery boiler considered the heart of an integrated pulp and paper mill? (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A (a):

The integrated Kraft mill consists of **four major process sections**:

1. **Wood Preparation Section:** Logs are debarked in a rotating drum, then chipped in disc chippers to 15–25 mm size. Chips are screened to remove fines, oversized chips, and knots.

2. **Pulping (Digestion) Section:** Screened chips are charged into a **continuous digester** along with white liquor (NaOH + Na₂S). Cooking is performed at **160–170 °C, 7–10 bar, for 1.5–2.5 hours (H-factor ≈ 1000–2000)**. The product is **brown stock** (cellulose-rich fibres) and **black liquor** (lignin-rich spent liquor).

3. **Bleaching Section:** Brown stock is washed and bleached in a multi-stage sequence (e.g., D₀ → E/P → D₁ → D₂) using **chlorine dioxide, oxygen, peroxide, and sodium hydroxide** to achieve target brightness of **88–92% ISO**.

4. **Paper Machine Section:** Bleached pulp is refined, diluted with white water to **0.8–1.2% consistency**, fed to a **headbox**, formed on a **Fourdrinier wire**, dewatered by vacuum and press rolls, dried over **steam-heated cylinders (40–60)**, calendered, and reeled.

> **Block diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete mill process block diagram with all inter-stage waste streams.

**Key Environmental Connection:** Each section generates characteristic emissions, summarized in the **Concept Sheet (Section 2.2)**.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A (b):

The Kraft process is commercially viable ONLY because of the **chemical recovery loop**, which transforms what would otherwise be a massive waste stream (black liquor) into **energy, recovered cooking chemicals, and lime**.

**Stages of Chemical Recovery:**

| Stage | Process | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Evaporation** | Multi-effect evaporator concentrates weak black liquor (15% solids) to strong black liquor (65–75% solids) | Concentrated liquor + condensate |
| **Recovery Boiler** | Strong black liquor combusted at **~1000 °C** in a Tomlinson boiler | Steam (energy) + smelt (Na₂CO₃ + Na₂S) + TRS emissions |
| **Smelt Dissolution** | Smelt dissolved in weak wash water | **Green liquor** (Na₂CO₃ + Na₂S solution) |
| **Causticizing** | Green liquor + slaked lime (Ca(OH)₂) | **White liquor** (regenerated cooking chemical) + **lime mud** (CaCO₃) |
| **Lime Reburning** | Lime mud calcined at ~900 °C in lime kiln | **Reburned lime (CaO)** for reuse |

**Stoichiometry of Causticizing:**

$$ \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{NaOH} + \text{CaCO}_3 $$

**Why the Recovery Boiler is the "Heart":**
1. **Energy source:** Converts the chemical energy of dissolved lignin (organics) into high-pressure steam — supplying **40–60%** of total mill energy.
2. **Chemical regeneration:** Reduces Na₂SO₄ (make-up salt) to Na₂S, regenerating active cooking agent.
3. **Waste minimization:** Eliminates the need to discharge black liquor — converting it into reusable energy and chemicals.
4. **Environmental performance:** Determines the mill’s TRS, SO₂, NO_x, and particulate emissions; well-operated recovery boilers achieve **TRS < 5 ppm**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often write that the recovery boiler "burns black liquor" without mentioning its **dual function of chemical regeneration (smelt) and energy recovery (steam)**. A 7-mark question requires both the *process flow* AND the *engineering rationale*. Marks are awarded for stating the temperature (~1000 °C), the reducing environment, and the dual product stream.

**CO Mapping:** CO3 (Analyze) | **Bloom's Level:** L4/L5

---

### Question B — Cleaner Production & Wastewater Management

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OECHT831, Module 3, Full 14 Marks]`

**Q. (a)** List the **major wastewater streams** generated in a Kraft pulp and paper mill and discuss the characteristic **pollutants** in each. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** Describe the **typical effluent treatment scheme** adopted in modern pulp and paper mills. How does it relate to the ISO 14001 framework? (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B (a):

| Stream | Origin | Characteristic Pollutants |
|---|---|---|
| **Debarking wastewater** | Log debarking drum | Suspended solids, tannins, extractives, BOD 200–500 mg/L |
| **Pulp washing filtrate** | Brown stock washers | High COD (5,000–15,000 mg/L), colour, lignin derivatives |
