| **Bleach plant effluent** | D₀, E/P, D₁, D₂ stages | AOX, chloroform, colour, acidic pH (2–4) |
| **Condensates** | Evaporator / digester | Methanol, ethanol, terpenes, TRS compounds |
| **White water overflow** | Paper machine | Suspended solids, starch, sizing agents, high COD |
| **Cleaning & blow-down** | Causticizing, lime kiln | High pH, calcium, suspended solids |
| **Utility wastewater** | Cooling tower, boiler blow-down | High temperature, hardness, biocides |

> [!NOTE]
> **Composite effluent** (combined) typically has:
> - Flow: **30–60 m³/t pulp**
> - COD: **800–1500 mg/L**
> - BOD: **300–600 mg/L**
> - AOX: **<2 mg/L** (modern ECF)
> - Colour: **500–2000 PCU**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B (b):

**Typical Effluent Treatment Scheme (Sequential):**

**Stage 1 — Primary Treatment:**
- Sedimentation / clarification in large circular clarifiers.
- Removal of settleable suspended solids (40–60% SS removal).
- **Sludge → sludge thickener → dewatering → landfill or land application.**

**Stage 2 — Secondary (Biological) Treatment:**
- **Aerobic activated sludge** with aeration tank (HRT 6–12 h) and secondary clarifier.
- Microbial degradation of dissolved organics (BOD/COD removal 80–95%).
- Optional: **Anaerobic UASB** pre-treatment for high-strength streams to recover biogas.

**Stage 3 — Tertiary Treatment (for strict discharge norms):**
- **Sand filtration / microfiltration** for residual SS.
- **Activated carbon adsorption** for residual colour and AOX.
- **UV / chlorination** for disinfection.

**Stage 4 — Sludge Management:**
- Thickening → Dewatering (belt press / centrifuge) → Disposal.

**Connection to ISO 14001 EMS:**

| ISO 14001 Clause | Implementation in Mill |
|---|---|
| **4.3.1 Environmental Aspects** | Aspect register identifies wastewater, TRS, AOX, solid waste |
| **4.3.2 Legal & Other Requirements** | Compliance with Water Act 1974, EPA 1986, CPCB/SPCB norms |
| **4.3.3 Objectives & Targets** | Reduction targets: 20% COD reduction, 50% water reuse in 2 years |
| **4.4.6 Operational Control** | SOPs for ETP operation, chemical dosing, sludge handling |
| **4.5.1 Monitoring & Measurement** | Online COD, pH, flow meters; daily SS and BOD analysis |
| **4.5.2 Evaluation of Compliance** | Internal audits against consent conditions; SPCB reporting |
| **4.5.3 Nonconformity, CAPA** | Corrective actions for effluent excursions (e.g., toxicity to fish) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> A common mistake is to **list treatment unit operations without explaining the ISO 14001 linkage**. Marks are split as: 4 marks for treatment scheme, 3 marks for ISO 14001 mapping. Drawing a flowchart (similar to Section 4.3) earns 1 additional mark.

**CO Mapping:** CO4 (Evaluate) | **Bloom's Level:** L5

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] The **Kraft process** dominates global chemical pulping because of strong fibres and closed chemical loop.
- [ ] **Black liquor** is BOTH a major waste stream AND a valuable energy/chemical resource — recovery boiler is the heart.
- [ ] **ECF bleaching** (chlorine dioxide) replaces Cl₂ → AOX reduced by ~70%; **TCF** uses only O₂/H₂O₂/O₃.
- [ ] **TRS (Total Reduced Sulfur)** = H₂S + CH₃SH + DMS + DMDS — the signature odor of pulp mills.
- [ ] **Kappa number** measures residual lignin → drives bleaching chemical demand.
- [ ] **H-factor** = combined time-temperature index for digester control.
- [ ] **Specific water consumption** for modern mills = **30–60 m³/t pulp**; target reduction via white water closure.
- [ ] **AOX** is the regulated chlorinated-organic indicator; modern ECF mills achieve **<0.25 kg AOX/t**.
- [ ] **Cleaner Production Hierarchy:** Source reduction > Internal reuse > Recycling > Treatment > Disposal.
- [ ] **ISO 14001 Clause 4.3.1 (Aspects)** → Aspect register is the foundation of any pulp & paper EMS.
- [ ] **Key Indian regulations** relevant: Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act 1974, Air Act 1981, EPA 1986, Forest Conservation Act 1980, Hazardous Waste Rules 2016.
- [ ] **Composite effluent** characteristics: COD 800–1500 mg/L, BOD 300–600 mg/L, AOX < 2 mg/L.
- [ ] **Recovery boiler** operates at ~1000 °C; smelt → green liquor → white liquor (NaOH + Na₂S).
- [ ] **Causticizing reaction:** Na₂CO₃ + Ca(OH)₂ → 2 NaOH + CaCO₃ (lime mud reburned at ~900 °C).
- [ ] **O₂ delignification** is a key cleaner-production insert between cooking and bleaching — reduces bleach demand by ~30–40%.
- [ ] **Numerical skill required:** Black liquor evaporation load, COD load per tonne, ClO₂ demand from Kappa number.
- [ ] **Closed-cycle mills ("zero-effluent" mills)** exist but have corrosion and salt-build-up challenges — discuss trade-offs.
- [ ] **Brown stock washing efficiency** is measured as **"dilution factor"** — modern target < 2.5 m³/t.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:**
> For a 14-mark Module 3 question, examiners expect: (1) a process block diagram, (2) identification of environmental aspects at each stage, (3) control technology / treatment sequence, and (4) an explicit ISO 14001 or legal-compliance linkage. Memorize the H-factor, Kappa number, AOX target, and the causticizing reaction — these are the four most frequently asked core facts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electroplating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT831 - ENVIRONMENT MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electroplating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Electroplating** is an **electrochemical surface engineering process** in which a thin, adherent, and uniform layer of a desired metal (the *deposit*) is deposited onto the surface of a conductive substrate (the *cathode*) through the controlled reduction of metal ions from an aqueous electrolyte solution, using an external direct current (DC) power source. The process is governed by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis** and is widely employed in engineering to enhance **corrosion resistance, surface hardness, electrical conductivity, solderability, reflectivity, and aesthetic appeal** of base materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OECHT831 – Module 3):**
> Electroplating is studied under the umbrella of **Industrial Wastewater Sources and Treatment**, because electroplating industries are categorized as one of the most polluting sectors due to the generation of wastewater laden with **heavy metals (Cr⁶⁺, Ni²⁺, Cd²⁺, Pb²⁺, Cu²⁺, Zn²⁺)**, **cyanides**, and **acid/alkaline effluents**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to gift-wrap a steel ball with a thin golden foil. You cannot physically wrap it because the foil is too thin. So instead, you dissolve the gold in a special chemical bath (the electrolyte) and pass electricity through it. The gold atoms, now floating as charged particles in the liquid, get "tricked" into climbing onto the steel ball and settling as a perfectly uniform, bonded metallic skin. This is essentially what **electroplating** does — it uses **electricity as a tool to reassemble metal atoms** from a solution onto a target object.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:**
> In electroplating, **electrons travel through the wire**, but **ions travel through the solution**. The anode *dissolves* (sacrificial), while the cathode (workpiece) *gains* metal. This is the **opposite** of an electrolytic refining cell where the impurity-laden anode is the impure metal.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Faraday's Constant | F | **96,485 C·mol⁻¹** (≈ **96,500 C·mol⁻¹** for KTU calculations) |
| Standard Temperature | T | **25 °C (298.15 K)** |
| Standard Current Density Range | i | **10 – 50 A·m⁻²** (for decorative plating) |
| Plating Bath pH | pH | **0.5 – 14** (depending on metal) |
| Plating Thickness | δ | **5 – 25 µm (decorative)**, **25 – 250 µm (functional)** |
| Cathode Current Efficiency (η) | η | **60 % – 95 %** (typical industrial range) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Working Principle — A Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The electroplating operation can be broken down into **five sequential electrochemical stages**:

1. **Establishment of the Electrolytic Cell:** The workpiece (cathode) and a sacrificial metal anode are immersed in an aqueous electrolyte containing dissolved ions of the metal to be plated.
2. **Application of DC Potential:** An external DC power source drives electrons from the anode terminal to the cathode terminal through the external circuit.
3. **Anodic Dissolution (Oxidation):** At the anode, the sacrificial metal loses electrons and enters the solution as positive ions:
   $$M_{(s)} \longrightarrow M^{n+}_{(aq)} + ne^{-}$$
4. **Ionic Migration in Electrolyte:** Cations (Mⁿ⁺) migrate toward the cathode, while anions (e.g., SO₄²⁻, Cl⁻) migrate toward the anode, completing the internal ionic circuit.
5. **Cathodic Deposition (Reduction):** At the cathode surface, metal ions gain electrons and deposit as a coherent, adherent solid film:
   $$M^{n+}_{(aq)} + ne^{-} \longrightarrow M_{(s)}$$

### 2.2 The Core "Why" and "How" Behind Each Stage

- **Why is a DC current used and not AC?**
  Because AC alternates polarity 50/60 times per second, which would cause the freshly deposited layer to dissolve back during the negative half-cycle. DC provides a unidirectional electron flow necessary for sustained, directional reduction.
- **How is uniform thickness ensured?**
  Through control of **current density (i = I/A)**, **electrolyte agitation**, **conductivity**, **pH**, **temperature**, and the use of **additives (brighteners, levelers, wetting agents)**.
- **Why are complexing agents used?**
  They prevent the free metal ions from precipitating as hydroxides and help maintain a steady, controlled supply of ions at the cathode–electrolyte interface (e.g., **cyanide bath for gold/silver**, **sulfate bath for copper**).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Faraday's First Law** | $W = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | W = mass deposited (g); I = current (A); t = time (s); M = molar mass (g·mol⁻¹); n = valency; F = 96,485 C·mol⁻¹ | Calculates theoretical mass of metal plated |
| 2 | **Faraday's Second Law (Equivalent Weight Form)** | $W = \frac{E \cdot I \cdot t}{F}$ | E = Equivalent weight = M/n (g·eq⁻¹) | Quick mass calculation using electrochemical equivalents |
| 3 | **Cathode Current Efficiency (η)** | $\eta = \frac{W_{actual}}{W_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | η in %; W_actual = measured mass; W_theoretical = from Faraday's law | Measures process effectiveness; key for KTU problems |
| 4 | **Current Density (i)** | $i = \frac{I}{A}$ | i in A·m⁻²; I in A; A = cathode area (m²) | Determines plating rate, throw, and deposit quality |
| 5 | **Plating Thickness (δ)** | $\delta = \frac{W_{actual}}{\rho \cdot A}$ | δ in m; ρ = density of deposited metal (kg·m⁻³); A = area (m²) | Determines coating thickness and service life |
| 6 | **Plating Time Required** | $t = \frac{\delta \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M}$ | t in seconds | Time to achieve a target coating thickness |
| 7 | **Energy Consumption (E_eng)** | $E_{eng} = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{\eta}$ | V = cell voltage (V); E_eng in kWh | Economics of the plating process |
| 8 | **Quantity of Electricity (Q)** | $Q = I \cdot t = n \cdot F \cdot N$ | Q in Coulombs; N = moles of metal | Bridge between electrical and chemical quantities |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note for KTU Valuation:**
> Always use **F = 96,500 C·mol⁻¹** unless the question specifies otherwise. Marks are awarded only when the **correct unit conversions** (A·s = C, hours to seconds, cm² to m²) are explicitly shown.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Chrome plating on bumpers, pistons, and crankshafts for wear and corrosion resistance.
- **Electronics Industry:** Gold/silver plating on PCBs, connectors, and contacts for superior electrical conductivity.
- **Aerospace:** Nickel and cadmium plating on turbine components and fasteners.
- **Decorative Applications:** Jewellery, bathroom fittings, kitchenware.
- **Environmental Engineering Context:** Designing **end-of-pipe treatment** for **plating shop effluents** (Cr⁶⁺ reduction, cyanide oxidation, precipitation, ion exchange, and reverse osmosis).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Combined Plating Equation (Faraday's Law → Thickness)

The derivation integrates Faraday's law of electrolysis with geometric thickness. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly for KTU full-mark valuation.

**Given:** A cathode of area A (m²) is plated for time t (s) at a current I (A), with cathode current efficiency η. The metal being deposited has molar mass M (g·mol⁻¹), valency n, and density ρ (kg·m⁻³).

**Step 1 — Start with Faraday's First Law:**

$$W = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce cathode current efficiency (η), which represents the fraction of total charge actually used for the desired metal deposition:**

$$W_{actual} = \eta \cdot W_{theoretical} = \eta \cdot \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 3 — Convert mass to volume using the metal density (ρ):**

$$V = \frac{W_{actual}}{\rho} = \frac{\eta \cdot I \cdot t \cdot M}{\rho \cdot n \cdot F}$$

**Step 4 — Convert volume to coating thickness (δ) by dividing by the cathode area (A). Note the unit consistency: W in kg, ρ in kg·m⁻³, gives V in m³, divided by A in m² gives δ in m:**

$$\delta = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{\eta \cdot I \cdot t \cdot M}{\rho \cdot n \cdot F \cdot A}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to solve for the **time required to achieve a desired plating thickness δ**:**

$$t = \frac{\delta \cdot \rho \cdot n \cdot F \cdot A}{\eta \cdot I \cdot M}$$

**Step 6 — Express current in terms of current density (i = I/A):**

$$t = \frac{\delta \cdot \rho \cdot n \cdot F}{\eta \cdot i \cdot M}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Master Equation** for KTU electroplating numericals. Memorize its dimensional logic, not just the formula.

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem:** A steel sheet of area **0.25 m²** is to be electroplated with **nickel**. The plating bath operates at a current density of **300 A·m⁻²** with a cathode current efficiency of **90 %**. The required coating thickness is **30 µm**. Given: Atomic mass of Ni = **58.7 g·mol⁻¹**, valency n = **2**, density of Ni = **8900 kg·m⁻³**, F = **96,500 C·mol⁻¹**.
Calculate:
**(a)** The plating time required.
**(b)** The actual mass of nickel deposited.

---

#### Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1 — Identify and convert all quantities to SI units:**

$$\delta = 30 \, \mu m = 30 \times 10^{-6} \, m$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the total current from current density:**

$$I = i \cdot A = 300 \, A/m^2 \times 0.25 \, m^2 = 75 \, A$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Master Equation for plating time:**

$$t = \frac{\delta \cdot \rho \cdot n \cdot F}{\eta \cdot i \cdot M}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values (keeping M in kg·mol⁻¹ = 58.7/1000 = 0.0587 kg·mol⁻¹ for SI consistency, and noting η = 0.90):**

$$t = \frac{(30 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8900 \times 2 \times 96500}{0.90 \times 300 \times 0.0587}$$

**Step 5 — Compute numerator:**

$$(30 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8900 = 0.267$$

$$0.267 \times 2 = 0.534$$

$$0.534 \times 96500 = 51{,}531$$

**Step 6 — Compute denominator:**

$$0.90 \times 300 = 270$$

$$270 \times 0.0587 = 15.849$$

**Step 7 — Final division:**

$$t = \frac{51{,}531}{15.849} = 3251.5 \, s \approx 54.2 \, min$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 3252 \, s \approx 54.2 \, minutes}$$

---

#### Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1 — Apply Faraday's First Law with current efficiency:**

$$W_{actual} = \frac{\eta \cdot I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (M = 0.0587 kg·mol⁻¹):**

$$W_{actual} = \frac{0.90 \times 75 \times 3251.5 \times 0.0587}{2 \times 96500}$$

**Step 3 — Compute stepwise:**

$$0.90 \times 75 = 67.5$$

$$67.5 \times 3251.5 = 219{,}476.25$$

$$219{,}476.25 \times 0.0587 = 12{,}883.25$$

$$2 \times 96500 = 193{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Final division:**

$$W_{actual} = \frac{12{,}883.25}{193{,}000} = 0.06675 \, kg = 66.75 \, g$$

$$\boxed{W_{actual} \approx 66.75 \, g \, of \, nickel}$$

### 3.3 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix — Common Plating Baths

| Metal Plated | Electrolyte Composition | Operating pH | Cathode Reaction | Anode Material | Environmental Hazard |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Copper (Cu)** | CuSO₄ + H₂SO₄ | Acidic (0.5–1.0) | Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu | Pure Cu | Cu²⁺ toxicity to aquatic life |
| **Nickel (Ni)** | NiSO₄ + NiCl₂ + H₃BO₃ | 3.5–4.5 | Ni²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Ni | Pure Ni | Ni²⁺ carcinogen, dermatitis |
| **Chromium (Cr)** | CrO₃ + H₂SO₄ | Strongly acidic (<1) | Cr⁶⁺ + 6e⁻ → Cr | Pb (insoluble) | **Cr⁶⁺ is a Group 1 human carcinogen** |
| **Zinc (Zn)** | ZnSO₄ or ZnCl₂ | 3.5–4.5 | Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Zn | Pure Zn | Zn²⁺ moderate toxicity |
| **Cadmium (Cd)** | CdO + NaCN + NaOH | Alkaline (12+) | Cd²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cd | Pure Cd | **Cd²⁺ is bioaccumulative, ITLC** |
| **Gold (Au)** | KAu(CN)₂ + KCN | Alkaline (8–11) | Au⁺ + e⁻ → Au | Pure Au | Free cyanide (CN⁻) highly toxic |
| **Silver (Ag)** | KAg(CN)₂ + KCN | Alkaline (8–11) | Ag⁺ + e⁻ → Ag | Pure Ag | Free cyanide (CN⁻) highly toxic |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Electroplating Cell — Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[DC Power Supply Positive Terminal] --> B1[Anode Sacrificial Metal M]
    B1 --> C1[Anodic Dissolution Oxidation Reaction]
    C1 --> D1[Electrolyte Bath Containing Mn Ions and Acid or Cyanide]
    D1 --> E1[Ionic Migration Mn plus moves to cathode, Anions move to anode]
    E1 --> F1[Cathode Workpiece Substrate]
    F1 --> G1[Cathodic Reduction Deposition Mn plus plus ne minus to M]
    G1 --> H1[Coherent Metal Film on Substrate]
    H1 --> A2[DC Power Supply Negative Terminal]
    A2 --> A1
    D1 --> I1[Rinse Water Stage 1 Drag Out Recovery]
    I1 --> J1[Wastewater Treatment Cr6 Reduction, CN Oxidation, pH Neutralization]
    J1 --> K1[Sludge Dewatering and Safe Disposal]
    H1 --> L1[Quality Control Thickness Test, Adhesion Test, Porosity Test]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow of Electroplating Wastewater Treatment (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Plating Shop Effluent Stream] --> P2[Equalization Tank pH and Flow Stabilization]
    P2 --> P3[Hexavalent Chromium Reduction FeSO4 plus H2SO4 to Cr3 plus]
    P3 --> P4[Cyanide Oxidation Alkaline Chlorination CN to CO2 and N2]
    P4 --> P5[Heavy Metal Precipitation Lime or NaOH to Metal Hydroxides]
    P5 --> P6[Settler and Lamella Clarifier Sludge Separation]
    P6 --> P7[Sand Filter and Activated Carbon Polishing]
    P7 --> P8[Treated Effluent Discharge as per PCB Norms]
    P6 --> P9[Sludge Thickener and Filter Press]
    P9 --> P10[Landfill Encapsulation or Cement Stabilization]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram of Current Efficiency Concept (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Total Current I in A] --> R1[Useful Work Cathode Metal Deposition]
    Q1 --> R2[Parasitic Losses Hydrogen Evolution, Side Reactions]
    R1 --> S1[Actual Mass Deposited Wactual]
    R2 --> S2[Wasted Charge]
    S1 --> T1[Cathode Current Efficiency eta equals Wactual divided by Wtheoretical times 100]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define electroplating. List any four engineering applications of electroplated components.*

**Model Answer:**
Electroplating is an **electrochemical surface treatment process** in which a thin, adherent, and uniform metallic coating is deposited on a conductive substrate (cathode) by passing a direct current through an electrolyte solution containing ions of the metal to be deposited.

**Engineering Applications (any four):**
1. **Corrosion protection** — zinc or cadmium coatings on steel fasteners.
2. **Wear resistance** — hard chrome plating on hydraulic shafts and piston rings.
3. **Electrical conductivity** — gold/silver plating on PCB contacts and connectors.
4. **Decorative finishing** — nickel-chrome plating on automotive parts and bathroom fittings.
5. **Solderability** — tin plating on electronic leads.
6. **Reflectivity** — silver plating on mirrors and solar concentrators.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State Faraday's First and Second Laws of Electrolysis as applicable to electroplating operations.*

**Model Answer:**
**Faraday's First Law:** The mass (W) of a substance liberated or deposited at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity (Q) passed through the electrolyte.

$$W \propto Q = I \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad W = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Faraday's Second Law:** When the same quantity of electricity is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of substances deposited or liberated are proportional to their **chemical equivalent weights (E)**.

$$W \propto E \quad \Rightarrow \quad W = \frac{E \cdot Q}{F}$$

In KTU context, these laws form the **quantitative foundation** for calculating plating time, current, and coating thickness.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choice Pattern (14 Marks Each)

---

#### ✦ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **working principle of an electroplating cell**. Discuss the role of **anode, cathode, electrolyte, and current density** in determining the quality of the deposit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

In an electroplating cell, two electrodes — the **anode** (positive electrode, made of the pure metal to be deposited) and the **cathode** (negative electrode, the workpiece to be coated) — are immersed in an **electrolyte** containing ions of the plating metal. A **DC power supply** drives electrons from the anode to the cathode through the external circuit.

**At the Anode (Oxidation):** The metal atoms of the anode lose electrons and dissolve into the solution as positive ions:
$$M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^{-}$$

**At the Cathode (Reduction):** The metal ions migrate to the cathode, gain electrons, and deposit as a thin, adherent layer:
$$M^{n+} + ne^{-} \rightarrow M$$

**Role of Key Parameters:**

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Anode** | Source of plating metal ions; maintains ion concentration; dissolves to replenish the bath. |
| **Cathode (Workpiece)** | Site of reduction and deposition; surface preparation (polishing, degreasing, acid pickling) is critical for adhesion. |
| **Electrolyte** | Conducts ionic current; provides metal ions; pH and conductivity control deposit morphology. |
| **Current Density (i)** | Low i → bright, fine-grained deposit; high i → coarse, burnt deposit with hydrogen evolution. |
| **Temperature** | Higher T → better ion diffusion but coarser crystals; lower T → smoother but slower plating. |
| **Agitation** | Prevents concentration polarization; ensures uniform ion supply at cathode. |

A well-controlled cell produces a deposit with **good adhesion, uniform thickness, low porosity, and desired mechanical properties**.

---

**(b)** A **brass component of total surface area 0.05 m²** is to be electroplated with **silver** to a thickness of **20 µm**. The plating is carried out using a **cyanide bath** at a current density of **100 A·m⁻²** with a cathode efficiency of **85 %**. Given: Atomic mass of Ag = **108 g·mol⁻¹**, valency = 1, density of Ag = **10,500 kg·m⁻³**, F = **96,500 C·mol⁻¹**.
Calculate: **(i)** Plating time, **(ii)** Actual mass of silver deposited. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Plating Time:**

Using the master equation:
$$t = \frac{\delta \cdot \rho \cdot n \cdot F}{\eta \cdot i \cdot M}$$

Substituting (M = 0.108 kg·mol⁻¹, δ = 20×10⁻⁶ m):

$$t = \frac{(20 \times 10^{-6}) \times 10500 \times 1 \times 96500}{0.85 \times 100 \times 0.108}$$

**Numerator:** $20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10500 = 0.21$; $0.21 \times 96500 = 20{,}265$

**Denominator:** $0.85 \times 100 \times 0.108 = 9.18$

$$t = \frac{20265}{9.18} = 2207.5 \, s \approx 36.79 \, min$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 36.8 \, minutes}$$

**(ii) Actual Mass Deposited:**

First, total current: $I = i \times A = 100 \times 0.05 = 5 \, A$

$$W_{actual} = \frac{\eta \cdot I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{0.85 \times 5 \times 2207.5 \times 0.108}{1 \times 96500}$$

$$= \frac{0.85 \times 5 \times 2207.5 \times 0.108}{96500} = \frac{1013.4}{96500} \approx 0.0105 \, kg$$

$$\boxed{W_{actual} \approx 10.5 \, g \, of \, silver}$$

---

#### ✦ QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Discuss the **environmental impacts of electroplating industry** with respect to wastewater generation. Explain the **treatment sequence** for plating shop effluents containing **hexavalent chromium and free cyanide**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Environmental Impacts of Electroplating Wastewater:**

1. **Heavy Metal Toxicity:** Effluents contain Cr⁶⁺, Ni²⁺, Cd²⁺, Cu²⁺, Zn²⁺, Pb²⁺ — all toxic, bioaccumulative, and carcinogenic (especially Cr⁶⁺).
2. **Cyanide Poisoning:** Cyanide-bearing rinse water (from Au, Ag, Cu, Zn plating) is lethal to aquatic life and humans (CN⁻ inhibits cytochrome oxidase).
3. **Acid/Alkaline Burns:** pH extremes in waste streams damage sewers and aquatic ecosystems.
4. **Sludge Disposal Problem:** Metal hydroxide sludges from precipitation are classified as **hazardous waste** under Indian Hazardous Waste Rules.
5. **Groundwater Contamination:** Untreated seepage from effluent lagoons contaminates aquifers.

**Treatment Sequence for Cr⁶⁺ and CN⁻:**

| Step | Process | Chemistry | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Equalization** | Mixing tank | Stabilizes pH and flow fluctuations |
| 2 | **Cr⁶⁺ Reduction** | FeSO₄ + H₂SO₄ at pH 2–3 | $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + 6\text{Fe}^{2+} + 14\text{H}^+ \rightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 6\text{Fe}^{3+} + 7\text{H}_2\text{O}$ |
| 3 | **CN⁻ Oxidation (Alkaline Chlorination)** | Cl₂ + NaOH at pH > 10 | $\text{CN}^- + \text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{CNO}^- + 2\text{Cl}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ |
| 4 | **pH Neutralization** | Lime (Ca(OH)₂) to pH 8–9 | Precipitates Cr(OH)₃, Ni(OH)₂, Cu(OH)₂ |
| 5 | **Sedimentation/Clarification** | Lamella clarifier | Sludge removal |
| 6 | **Tertiary Polishing** | Sand filter + activated carbon | Final polishing |
| 7 | **Sludge Stabilization** | Cement/lime encapsulation | Safe landfill disposal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Order matters:** Cr⁶⁺ reduction must be done **before** cyanide oxidation if both are present, because Cr⁶⁺ re-oxidizes CN⁻ to less toxic cyanate.

---

**(b)** Define **cathode current efficiency**. A current of **20 A** is passed through a copper sulfate bath for **45 minutes**, depositing **17.8 g of copper**. Calculate: **(i)** Cathode current efficiency, **(ii)** Energy consumed if cell voltage is **3.5 V**. Given: Atomic mass of Cu = **63.5 g·mol⁻¹**, valency = 2, F = **96,500 C·mol⁻¹**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Cathode current efficiency (η) is the ratio of the actual mass of metal deposited at the cathode to the theoretical mass that *should* have been deposited according to Faraday's law, expressed as a percentage.

**(i) Cathode Current Efficiency:**

Time conversion: $t = 45 \times 60 = 2700 \, s$

**Theoretical mass** (Faraday's First Law):
$$W_{theoretical} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{20 \times 2700 \times 63.5}{2 \times 96500}$$

$$= \frac{3{,}429{,}000}{193{,}000} = 17.77 \, g$$

**Current Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{W_{actual}}{W_{theoretical}} \times 100 = \frac{17.8}{17.77} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 100.16 \% \approx 100 \%}$$

*(Note: Slight excess over 100% is usually due to weighing errors or inclusion of electrolyte salts; if exact data gives 17.8 g, this is essentially unity efficiency.)*

**(ii) Energy Consumed:**

$$E_{eng} = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{\eta} = \frac{3.5 \times 20 \times 2700}{1.0 \times 3600 \times 1000} \, kWh$$

(where 1 kWh = 3,600,000 J = 3.6 × 10⁶ J)

$$= \frac{189{,}000}{3{,}600{,}000} = 0.0525 \, kWh$$

$$\boxed{E_{eng} = 0.0525 \, kWh = 52.5 \, Wh}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch is the #1 mark killer:** Always convert µm → m, hours → seconds, cm² → m², g·mol⁻¹ → kg·mol⁻¹ before substituting into the master equation.
> 2. **Forgetting current efficiency (η):** If the question gives η, you **must** include it. Omitting it typically costs **2–3 marks** out of 7.
> 3. **Using wrong F:** KTU accepts both 96,485 and 96,500. Stick to one consistently.
> 4. **Mixing up anode and cathode reactions:** Students often write the oxidation at the cathode. Use the mnemonic — **"Red Cat, An Ox"** → **Reduction at Cathode, Oxidation at Anode**.
> 5. **Forgetting wastewater treatment sequence:** Cr⁶⁺ must be reduced **before** CN⁻ oxidation — writing them in reverse order loses full process marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **Electroplating** = Electrochemical deposition of a metal coating using **DC current**, governed by **Faraday's laws**.
- ☐ **Faraday's Constant (F)** = **96,500 C·mol⁻¹** (MUST remember).
- ☐ **Master Equation**: $t = \dfrac{\delta \cdot \rho \cdot n \cdot F}{\eta \cdot i \cdot M}$ — memorize its **derivation logic**, not just the form.
- ☐ **Cathode Current Efficiency (η)** = (Actual mass / Theoretical mass) × 100; typical range 60–95 %.
- ☐ **Anode** = sacrificial, dissolves; **Cathode** = workpiece, gains metal.
- ☐ **Anionic rule:** **"Red Cat, An Ox"** — Reduction at Cathode, Oxidation at Anode.
- ☐ **Plating bath types:** Acid sulfate (Cu), Watts bath (Ni), chromic acid (Cr), cyanide (Au, Ag, Cd).
- ☐ **Environmental hazards:** Cr⁶⁺ (Group 1 carcinogen), CN⁻ (lethal), heavy metals (bioaccumulative).
- ☐ **Treatment sequence:** Equalization → Cr⁶⁺ reduction (FeSO₄) → CN⁻ oxidation (Cl₂) → pH neutralization (lime) → precipitation → sludge disposal.
- ☐ **Order rule:** Reduce **Cr⁶⁺ first**, then oxidize **CN⁻**.
- ☐ **Key equation connections:** $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ is the **starting point** for almost every KTU electroplating numerical.
- ☐ **Plating thickness** typically 5–25 µm (decorative) and 25–250 µm (functional).
- ☐ **Current density (i)** controls deposit quality: low i = smooth/bright; high i = coarse/burnt.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tanning Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT831 - ENVIRONMENT MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tanning Industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Tanning Industry (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> The **Tanning Industry** is a chemical-intensive manufacturing sector that processes **raw animal hides and skins** (from cattle, buffalo, sheep, goat, and pig) into durable, flexible, and non-putrescible **leather** through a sequence of **beam-house**, **tanyard**, **re-tanning**, **dyeing**, and **finishing** operations. From an environmental management perspective, it is classified as one of the **most polluting industrial sectors** worldwide due to the generation of high-strength wastewater containing **heavy metals (Cr³⁺), sulfides, chlorides, ammonia-nitrogen, dissolved solids, and high organic load**, alongside solid wastes such as **fleshings, chrome shavings, buffing dust, and sludge**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OECHT831 – Module 3)**
> Under the KTU 2024 OBE framework, this topic is mapped to **CO3**: *"Analyse the environmental impacts of major process industries and recommend appropriate pollution-control strategies."* Examiners frequently test process flow, effluent characterization, chromium recovery, and **Common Effluent Treatment Plant (CETP)** design logic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Kitchen Curry" Analogy 🍛**
> Think of raw animal skin as a **perishable ingredient** (like raw fish) — if you simply leave it in the sun, it rots within hours due to bacterial and enzymatic action. **Tanning is essentially "pickling and preserving"** the skin by cross-linking its protein (collagen) fibers so they never rot, even when wet. Just as a chef uses **turmeric, salt, vinegar, and spices** to preserve food, the tanner uses **chromium salts, vegetable tannins (from tree bark), acids, and alkalis** to lock the collagen structure. The catch? Every spice leaves a residue — and the **spice washings (effluent)** are extremely strong, colored, toxic, and salty. The tanner's challenge is to *make good leather without contaminating the river*.

## 1.3 Significance of the Tanning Industry

| Parameter | Global / Indian Context |
|---|---|
| Global leather production | **~23 billion sq. ft/year** |
| India's share in global production | **~13%** (2nd largest producer after China) |
| India's export earnings from leather | **USD 5–6 billion/year** |
| Employment in India | **~4.5 million workers** (mostly in Tamil Nadu, UP, WB, **Kerala**) |
| Water consumed per kg of hide processed | **17–35 litres** (process water) + washing |
| Wastewater generated per kg raw hide | **25–80 litres** |
| Major Kerala clusters | **Kottayam, Ernakulam, Thrissur (small-scale)** |

## 1.4 Why Tanning is a "Red Category" Industry

> [!WARNING]
> The **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** classifies tanneries as **17-category highly polluting industries** (Red Category). In Kerala, the State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB) monitors small-scale clusters under the **Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991** and the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**.

**Reasons for the "Red" tag:**
- High **BOD₅** (2,000–5,000 mg/L) — raw effluent strength
- High **COD** (5,000–50,000 mg/L)
- **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** up to 50,000 mg/L (salt-laden)
- **Chromium (Cr³⁺)** concentration of 50–500 mg/L in spent chrome liquor
- **Sulfide (S²⁻)** concentration of 200–800 mg/L in deliming effluent
- Offensive **odour** from H₂S and mercaptans
- **Solid waste** generation: ~50% of raw hide weight becomes solid waste
- **Heavy metal** contamination of groundwater

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Raw Materials and Hides/Skins Used

| Category | Examples | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Hides** (large animals) | Cow, buffalo, ox | Heavy leather (shoe soles, belts, industrial) |
| **Skins** (small animals) | Sheep, goat, pig, calf | Light leather (garments, gloves, upholstery) |
| **Pelt** | Wool-on sheep skin | Fur/leather garments |

> [!NOTE]
> **Composition of raw hide:** ~**64% water**, **33% protein** (mainly **collagen**), **2% fat**, **0.5% mineral salts**, **0.5% other substances**. The **collagen** is the structural protein that tanning aims to stabilize.

## 2.2 Stages of Leather Manufacture — Sequential Logic

The tanning process is conventionally divided into **four major departments**:

### **A. Beam-House Operations (Pre-Tanning)**

1. **Soaking** — Rehydrates cured/salted hides using water + wetting agents + bactericides.
2. **Liming** — Hair removal and swelling using **Ca(OH)₂ + Na₂S** (4–6% on hide weight). Opens up collagen fiber structure.
3. **Fleshing** — Mechanical removal of subcutaneous tissue.
4. **Dehairing** — Already integrated in liming; produces hair as solid waste.
5. **Deliming** — Removes lime using **ammonium salts (NH₄Cl)** or weak acids; produces ammonia-laden wastewater.
6. **Bating** — Enzymatic softening using proteolytic enzymes (trypsin, pancreatin).
7. **Pickling** — Acidifies pelt using **H₂SO₄ + NaCl** to pH ~2.5–3.5 for chrome tanning compatibility.

### **B. Tanyard Operations**

8. **Tanning (Chrome/Vegetable/Alum)** — Cross-links collagen:
   - **Chrome tanning:** Basic chromium sulfate (Cr₂(SO₄)₃) — 33% basicity
   - **Vegetable tanning:** Polyphenols from **mimosa, quebracho, chestnut** bark
9. **Sammying / Setting** — Mechanical water removal (squeezing, vacuum).

### **C. Post-Tanning (Re-Tanning, Dyeing, Fatliquoring)**

10. **Re-tanning** — Adds fullness and grain tightness (vegetable + chrome + syntans).
11. **Neutralization** — Mild alkali (NaHCO₃) to pH ~5–6.
12. **Dyeing** — Acid, direct, or metal-complex dyes.
13. **Fatliquoring** — Emulsified oils/fats for softness and flexibility.

### **D. Finishing Operations**

14. **Drying** — Hang, paste, vacuum, or toggle drying.
15. **Conditioning** — Moisture equalization (~20%).
16. **Staking / Softening** — Mechanical stretching.
17. **Buffing** — Surface abrasion to produce **nubuck / suede**.
18. **Finishing coats** — Pigments, lacquers, polymers for aesthetics.

## 2.3 Types of Tanning — Comparative Analysis

| Tanning Type | Tanning Agent | pH Range | Time | Leather Character | Env. Burden |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Chrome (Mineral)** | Basic Cr₂(SO₄)₃ | 2.5–4.0 | 4–8 h | Soft, supple, heat-resistant, blue ("wet blue") | **High Cr³⁺, high TDS** |
| **Vegetable** | Tannins (polyphenols) | 3.0–5.0 | Days–weeks | Firm, brown, heavy, traditional | High BOD, color |
| **Alum (Mineral)** | Aluminium sulfate + NaCl | 4.0 | Hours | White, soft, but water-sensitive | Moderate salts |
| **Zirconium** | ZrOCl₂ / Zr(SO₄)₂ | 2.5–3.5 | Hours | White, like chrome | Zr residual |
| **Aldehyde (Glutaraldehyde)** | Aldehydes | 7.5–8.0 | Hours | White, perspiration-resistant | Low sludge |
| **Oil Tanning (Chamois)** | Marine/vegetable oils | — | Hours | Soft, absorbent | High oil COD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **~80–90% of world leather is chrome-tanned** due to speed, leather quality, and cost. Hence, **chromium management is the single most important environmental issue** in tanneries.

## 2.4 Environmental Emissions — Triple Bottom Line

| Stream | Pollutants | Source Stage |
|---|---|---|
| **Liquid Effluent** | High BOD/COD, Cr³⁺, S²⁻, NH₃-N, Cl⁻, Na⁺, SO₄²⁻, color, grease, pathogens | All stages — especially beam-house & tanyard |
| **Solid Waste** | Fleshings, hair, trimmings, chrome shavings, buffing dust, sludge, used chemicals | Beam-house, tanyard, finishing |
| **Air Emissions** | H₂S, NH₃, VOCs (solvents), particulate matter (buffing dust), odour | Liming, deliming, finishing, drying |
| **Sludge** | Mixed chemical + biological sludge with Cr³⁺ | ETP/CETP |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{D_1 - D_2}{P} \times 1000$ | 5-day BOD (dilution method) | mg/L |
| 2 | $\text{COD} = \dfrac{(B - S) \times N \times 8000}{V}$ | COD by dichromate open reflux | mg/L |
| 3 | $\% \text{ Chrome Exhaustion} = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | Chrome uptake in tanning | % |
| 4 | $\text{Cr Recovery (\%)} = \dfrac{m_{Cr,recovered}}{m_{Cr,fed}} \times 100$ | Mass balance on Cr | % |
| 5 | $Q_{eff} = q \times M$ | Daily effluent volume | L/day |
| 6 | $M_{sludge} = Y \times Q \times (S_0 - S_e)$ | Sludge mass from biological treatment | kg/day |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100\%$ | Treatment efficiency | % |
| 8 | $\text{NTU} \leftrightarrow \text{TSS (mg/L)} \approx 2 \times \text{NTU}$ | Turbidity–TSS correlation | — |
| 9 | $TKN = \text{Organic-N} + \text{NH}_3\text{-N} + \text{NO}_2\text{-N} + \text{NO}_3\text{-N}$ | Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen | mg/L |
| 10 | $\text{PME} = \dfrac{m_{waste}}{m_{product}}$ | Pollutant Mass Emission per unit product | kg/kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** For chromium, examiners often expect the equilibrium expression:
> $$\text{Cr}^{3+} + 3\text{OH}^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Cr(OH)}_3 \downarrow \quad (K_{sp} \approx 6.3 \times 10^{-31})$$
> Students writing the **log–log solubility diagram** of Cr(OH)₃ vs pH always score full marks.

## 2.6 Effluent Discharge Standards (CPCB / KSPCB Norms)

| Parameter | Inland Surface Water | Marine/Coastal | Public Sewers |
|---|---|---|---|
| pH | **6.5–8.5** | 6.5–9.0 | 6.5–8.5 |
| BOD₅ (mg/L) | **≤ 30** | 100 | 350 |
| COD (mg/L) | **≤ 250** | 250 | — |
| Total Suspended Solids | **≤ 100** | 100 | 600 |
| Total Chromium (as Cr) | **≤ 2.0 mg/L** | 2.0 | 2.0 |
| Sulfide (as S²⁻) | **≤ 1.0 mg/L** | 5.0 | — |
| TDS | **≤ 2100 mg/L** | — | 2100 |
| Ammonical Nitrogen | **≤ 50 mg/L** | 50 | 50 |
| Chloride | **≤ 1000 mg/L** | — | 1000 |

> [!WARNING]
> Kerala's **KSPCB** is **stricter** than CPCB in some parameters (especially for Cr, where state boards often insist on **≤ 0.5 mg/L** for inland discharges in ecologically sensitive areas like the Vembanad Lake catchment).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mass Balance on Chrome Liquor — Standard KTU Derivative

This is a **favourite KTU question** (often 5–7 marks). Let us derive the **theoretical chromium exhaustion and the sludge produced from neutralization**.

### **Problem Context**

A tannery processes **1000 kg of wet salted hide** per batch. The chrome tanning bath uses:
- Basic chromium sulfate (BCS) at **8% on shaved weight**
- Assumed wet shaved weight = **1000 kg** (after liming and de-hairing, pelt is ~double cured weight; assuming pelt weight = 1000 kg)
- BCS contains **25% Cr₂O₃ equivalent** (i.e., 33% basicity commercial product)
- Exhaustion efficiency of bath = **70%** (typical)
- Subsequent neutralization converts remaining Cr³⁺ to **Cr(OH)₃** precipitate using **NaOH (100% excess)**.

Find:
1. The **mass of Cr₂O₃ fed** to the bath.
2. The **mass of Cr fixed in leather**.
3. The **mass of Cr in spent liquor**.
4. The **mass of Cr(OH)₃ sludge** generated on neutralization.

### **Step-by-Step Solution**

**Step 1: Mass of BCS added**

$$m_{BCS} = 8\% \times 1000 \text{ kg} = 80 \text{ kg of BCS}$$

**Step 2: Mass of Cr₂O₃ equivalent in BCS**

Given commercial BCS contains 25% Cr₂O₃ by mass:

$$m_{Cr_2O_3} = 0.25 \times 80 = 20 \text{ kg of Cr}_2\text{O}_3$$

**Step 3: Convert Cr₂O₃ to equivalent Cr³⁺ mass**

The molecular weight ratio:

$$\frac{2 \times 52}{2 \times 52 + 3 \times 16} = \frac{104}{152} = 0.6842$$

Therefore:

$$m_{Cr} = 0.6842 \times 20 = 13.684 \text{ kg of Cr (as element)}$$

**Step 4: Mass of Cr exhausted into leather (uptake)**

At 70% exhaustion:

$$m_{Cr,fixed} = 0.70 \times 13.684 = 9.579 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5: Mass of Cr remaining in spent liquor**

$$m_{Cr,spent} = 0.30 \times 13.684 = 4.105 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6: Convert to Cr(OH)₃ for sludge calculation**

Molecular weight of Cr(OH)₃ = 52 + 3(17) = **103 g/mol**
Cr atomic mass = **52 g/mol**

Mass ratio:

$$\frac{Cr(OH)_3}{Cr} = \frac{103}{52} = 1.981$$

Mass of Cr(OH)₃ sludge from spent liquor (assuming all Cr in spent liquor precipitates on NaOH addition):

$$m_{Cr(OH)_3} = 4.105 \times 1.981 = 8.131 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 7: NaOH required (theoretical)**

Stoichiometry:

$$\text{Cr}^{3+} + 3\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{Cr(OH)}_3$$

Moles of Cr³⁺:

$$n_{Cr^{3+}} = \frac{4.105 \times 10^3}{52} = 78.94 \text{ mol}$$

Moles of OH⁻ required:

$$n_{OH^-} = 3 \times 78.94 = 236.8 \text{ mol}$$

Mass of NaOH:

$$m_{NaOH} = 236.8 \times 40 = 9473 \text{ g} \approx 9.47 \text{ kg (theoretical)}$$

With **100% excess**:

$$m_{NaOH,actual} = 2 \times 9.47 = 18.95 \text{ kg}$$

### **Summary Table — Chrome Mass Balance**

| Item | Mass (kg) | % of Cr input |
|---|---|---|
| Cr₂O₃ added (as 25% of BCS) | 20.00 | — |
| Equivalent Cr³⁺ in feed | 13.68 | 100% |
| Cr fixed in leather | 9.58 | 70% |
| Cr in spent liquor | 4.10 | 30% |
| Cr(OH)₃ sludge | 8.13 | — |
| NaOH consumed (100% excess) | 18.95 | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight — "Recovery vs Disposal" Trade-off**
> Modern tanneries **recover and reuse** the spent chrome liquor via:
> - **Direct reuse** (70% exhaustion already saves 70%)
> - **Magnesium oxide (MgO) precipitation** at pH 8–9 → re-dissolving in H₂SO₄ to regenerate basic chrome liquor
> - **Membrane filtration (UF/RO)** to concentrate spent liquor
> - This reduces fresh Cr requirement to as low as **3–4% on hide weight**, slashing sludge generation.

## 3.2 BOD/COD Mass Loading Calculation

### **Problem**

A tannery processes **5000 kg raw hide/day**. Wastewater generated = **30 L/kg hide**.  
Raw wastewater: BOD = **3500 mg/L**, COD = **7000 mg/L**, Cr³⁺ = **150 mg/L**.

Find:
1. Daily wastewater flow.
2. Daily BOD load.
3. Daily Cr³⁺ load.
4. Required ETP efficiency to meet discharge standard BOD = 30 mg/L.

### **Step-by-Step**

**Step 1: Daily flow Q**

$$Q = 30 \text{ L/kg} \times 5000 \text{ kg/day} = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ L/day} = 150 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 2: Daily BOD load**

$$L_{BOD} = C \times Q = 3500 \text{ mg/L} \times 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ L/day}$$

$$L_{BOD} = 5.25 \times 10^8 \text{ mg/day} = 525 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3: Daily Cr³⁺ load**

$$L_{Cr} = 150 \text{ mg/L} \times 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ L/day} = 2.25 \times 10^7 \text{ mg/day} = 22.5 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 4: Required ETP efficiency for BOD = 30 mg/L**

$$\eta_{BOD} = \frac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100\% = \frac{3500 - 30}{3500} \times 100\% = 99.14\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why tanneries require **multi-stage treatment**: primary sedimentation + biological (aerobic/anaerobic) + tertiary (for Cr and polishing). A simple septic tank or single-stage activated sludge **cannot** achieve 99%+ BOD removal.

## 3.3 Sulphide Removal — Oxidation Stoichiometry

Liming effluent contains sulfide (Na₂S + HS⁻). **Air oxidation** with Mn²⁺ catalyst is used:

$$2\text{HS}^- + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{S}_2\text{O}_3^{2-} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Or with **H₂O₂**:

$$\text{S}^{2-} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Molar ratio of H₂O₂ : S²⁻ = 4:1**

For 1000 mg/L S²⁻ in 100 m³:

$$n_{S^{2-}} = \frac{1000 \times 100 \times 10^3}{32} = 3.125 \times 10^6 \text{ mol}$$

H₂O₂ required (30% w/w, density 1.11 g/mL):

$$m_{H_2O_2} = 4 \times 3.125 \times 10^6 \times 34 = 4.25 \times 10^8 \text{ g} = 425 \text{ tonnes (pure)}$$

Mass of 30% solution:

$$m_{30\%} = \frac{425}{0.30} = 1416.7 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Cost implication** — H₂O₂ is expensive, so **recycling spent liming liquor** (closed-loop) is preferred to fresh chemical treatment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Process Flow Diagram — Tannery Operations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Hides and Skins Arrival] --> B[Curing - Salting or Drying]
    B --> C[Soaking Tank]
    C --> D[Liming with CaOH2 and Na2S]
    D --> E[Dehairing and Fleshing]
    E --> F[Deliming with NH4Cl]
    F --> G[Bating - Enzymatic]
    G --> H[Pickling with H2SO4 and NaCl]
    H --> I[Tanning - Chrome BCS 8 percent]
    I --> J[Sammying - Water Removal]
    J --> K[Splitting and Shaving]
    K --> L[Re-tanning and Neutralization]
    L --> M[Dyeing]
    M --> N[Fatliquoring]
    N --> O[Drying - Toggle or Vacuum]
    O --> P[Staking and Buffing]
    P --> Q[Finishing - Lacquer and Pigments]
    Q --> R[Finished Leather Dispatch]

    D -.->|Solid Waste| S1[Fleshings, Hair, Lime Sludge]
    I -.->|Liquid| S2[Spent Chrome Liquor - Cr]
    F -.->|Liquid| S3[Ammonia and Lime Effluent]
    H -.->|Liquid| S4[Pickle Effluent - High Salt]
    Q -.->|Air| S5[VOC and Particulate Emission]

    S1 --> W1[Solid Waste Management]
    S2 --> W2[Chrome Recovery Unit]
    S3 --> W3[Sulphide Oxidation Tank]
    S4 --> W4[Salt Recovery RO]
    S5 --> W5[Air Pollution Control]

    W1 --> CETP[Common Effluent Treatment Plant]
    W2 --> CETP
    W3 --> CETP
    W4 --> CETP
    W5 --> CETP

    CETP --> OUTFALL[Treated Effluent Discharge]
    CETP --> SLUDGE[Hazardous Sludge to TSDF]
```

## 4.2 Common Effluent Treatment Plant (CETP) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Raw Mixed Tannery Effluent] --> EQ[Equalization Tank]
    EQ --> SCR[Screen and Grit Removal]
    SCR --> NEU[Neutralization Basin pH 7 to 8]
    NEU --> CRSED[Chrome Recovery via Precipitation]
    CRSED --> CRSL[Chrome Sludge to Hazardous Waste]
    CRSL --> HAZW[TSDF Secured Landfill]

    CRSED --> CLAR1[Primary Clarifier]
    CLAR1 --> PSLD[Primary Sludge to HAZW]

    CLAR1 --> BIO[Aeration Tank ASP or SBR]
    BIO --> CLAR2[Secondary Clarifier]
    CLAR2 --> BIO_OUT[Secondary Biological Sludge]

    CLAR2 --> TER[Tertiary Treatment - Sand Filter]
    TER --> AC[Activated Carbon Adsorber]
    AC --> DIS[Disinfection Chlorination]
    DIS --> OUT[Discharge to Water Body or Land]

    BIO_OUT --> DG[Sludge Digester]
    DG --> DEW[Sludge Dewatering - Filter Press]
    DEW --> FINAL[Dewatered Sludge Cake to HAZW]
```

## 4.3 Chrome Recovery Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SPENT[Spent Chrome Liquor] --> FILT[Filtration - Remove Suspended Solids]
    FILT --> RAISE[Increase pH to 8.0 to 8.5 using MgO]
    RAISE --> PREC[CrOH3 Precipitate Forms]
    PREC --> SETTLE[Settling Tank]
    SETTLE --> FILT2[Filter Press - Dewater CrOH3 Cake]
    FILT2 --> DISS[Dissolve in H2SO4]
    DISS --> REGEN[Regenerated Basic Chrome Sulfate BCS]
    REGEN --> REUSE[Reuse in Tanning Bath]
    SETTLE --> SUP[Supernatant - Low Cr - Joins Main Stream]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `EQ`, `NEU`, `BIO`, `CLAR1`) and contain no markdown formatting inside labels, ensuring clean rendering in GitHub, VS Code, and KTU e-learning platforms.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

### **Q1. List the four major departments of leather manufacture. What is the role of the "beam-house"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four major departments are:
1. **Beam-house** (pre-tanning: soaking, liming, deliming, bating, pickling)
2. **Tanyard** (tanning itself)
3. **Post-tanning** (re-tanning, dyeing, fatliquoring)
4. **Finishing** (drying, staking, buffing, coating)

**Role of beam-house (1 mark):** It prepares the raw hide for tanning by **rehydrating**, **removing hair and epidermis**, **removing non-collagen proteins and fats**, and **adjusting pH**. It generates the **most polluting wastewater** of the entire process (high BOD, sulfide, lime, ammonia, salt).

---

### **Q2. What is chrome exhaustion? Why is high exhaustion important in tanneries?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Chrome exhaustion (1 mark):** The percentage of chromium offered in the tanning bath that is **absorbed and fixed** by the hide collagen. Mathematically, $\text{Exhaustion \%} = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$.
- **Importance (2 marks):**
  1. **Reduces Cr in spent liquor** → less sludge → lower disposal cost.
  2. **Saves chromium salt cost** (chrome is expensive — Rs 60–80/kg).
  3. **Reduces environmental burden** — Cr is a heavy metal and classified as hazardous.
  4. **Modern target: > 90% exhaustion** using float shortening, basification with MgO, and pickle-free chrome tanning.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

#### **(a) With a neat flow diagram, describe the various unit operations and unit processes involved in the manufacture of leather from raw hides. Highlight the chemicals used at each stage and the by-products generated. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**  
Leather manufacture converts perishable raw hides into durable, non-putrescible material through a sequence of chemical and mechanical operations grouped into **four departments**: Beam-house, Tanyard, Post-tanning, and Finishing.

**Process Flow with Chemicals & By-products (5 marks):**

| Stage | Chemicals Used | By-products / Wastes |
|---|---|---|
| Soaking | Water, wetting agents, bactericides | Salted wash water, dirt |
| Liming & Unhairing | Ca(OH)₂, Na₂S | Hair, lime sludge, sulfide effluent |
| Fleshing | (Mechanical) | Fleshings (fatty solid waste) |
| Deliming | NH₄Cl, CO₂ | Ammoniacal effluent |
| Bating | Enzymes (pancreatin) | Organic effluent |
| Pickling | H₂SO₄ + NaCl | Acidic, salty effluent |
| Chrome Tanning | Basic Cr₂(SO₃)₃ | Spent chrome liquor (Cr³⁺) |
| Re-tanning | Syntans, vegetable extracts | Coloured effluent |
| Dyeing | Acid/metal-complex dyes | Coloured effluent |
| Fatliquoring | Sulphated/neatfoot oils | Oily emulsion effluent |
| Finishing | Pigments, lacquers, casein | VOCs, dust |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**  
Each stage uses toxic/persistent chemicals, and a **CETP** with **segregation of spent chrome liquor** is essential for environmental compliance.

**Diagram:** Refer Section 4.1 flow chart for the master process flow.

---

#### **(b) A tannery processes 8 tonnes of raw hide per day. Wastewater generation is 35 L/kg of hide. The combined effluent has BOD = 4000 mg/L, Cr³⁺ = 200 mg/L, and sulfide = 600 mg/L. Calculate: (i) daily wastewater flow, (ii) daily BOD load, (iii) daily Cr³⁺ load, and (iv) minimum chrome sludge generated if 30% of Cr is exhausted and all the residual Cr is precipitated as Cr(OH)₃. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Daily wastewater flow Q:**

$$Q = 35 \text{ L/kg} \times 8000 \text{ kg/day} = 2{,}80{,}000 \text{ L/day} = 280 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**(ii) Daily BOD load:**

$$L_{BOD} = 4000 \text{ mg/L} \times 2{,}80{,}000 \text{ L/day} = 1.12 \times 10^9 \text{ mg/day} = 1120 \text{ kg/day}$$

**(iii) Daily Cr³⁺ load:**

$$L_{Cr} = 200 \text{ mg/L} \times 2{,}80{,}000 \text{ L/day} = 5.6 \times 10^7 \text{ mg/day} = 56 \text{ kg/day}$$

**(iv) Chrome sludge as Cr(OH)₃:**

Spent liquor carries **70% of Cr input** (since only 30% is exhausted).

$$m_{Cr,spent} = 0.70 \times 56 = 39.2 \text{ kg/day}$$

Convert to Cr(OH)₃ using mass ratio 103/52 = 1.981:

$$m_{Cr(OH)_3} = 39.2 \times 1.981 = 77.66 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Answers in one line:**
- (i) 280 m³/day
- (ii) 1120 kg BOD/day
- (iii) 56 kg Cr/day
- (iv) 77.66 kg Cr(OH)₃ sludge/day

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) Discuss in detail the wastewater treatment scheme for a tannery effluent. Include primary, secondary, and tertiary treatment with a neat block diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Need for Segregated Treatment (1 mark):**  
Tannery wastewater is a **mixture of high-strength process streams** (chrome, sulfide, ammonia, salts) and low-strength washing streams. **Segregation** (chrome liquor separately, sulfide liquor separately, general effluent) is the **key principle** for economical treatment.

**Treatment Scheme (5 marks):**

| Stage | Unit Operation / Process | Purpose | Key Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-treatment** | Screening, grit removal | Remove large solids | — |
| **Segregation** | Separate spent chrome, sulfide, and pickle streams | Avoid mixing toxic with general | Batch basis |
| **Chrome Recovery** | MgO precipitation, settling, re-dissolution | Recycle Cr, reduce sludge | pH 8.0–8.5 |
| **Sulphide Oxidation** | Air + Mn²⁺ catalyst OR H₂O₂ | Oxidize S²⁻ to S₂O₃²⁻ or SO₄²⁻ | ORP > 50 mV |
| **Equalization** | Buffer tank | Homogenize pH, flow, concentration | HRT 6–8 h |
| **Primary Clarification** | Settling tank | Remove suspended solids, lime sludge | Overflow rate < 1 m/h |
| **Secondary Biological** | Activated Sludge / SBR / UASB | BOD/COD reduction (90%+) | MLSS 3000–5000 mg/L |
| **Tertiary** | Sand filter + Activated carbon | Polishing, color, residual Cr | TSS < 10 mg/L |
| **Disinfection** | Chlorination / UV | Pathogen kill | — |
| **Sludge Handling** | Thickener + Filter press | Solid waste volume reduction | 25–30% dry solids |

**Advanced Options (1 mark):**  
- **Membrane Bioreactor (MBR)** for compact plants
- **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** for water reuse and salt recovery
- **Constructed Wetlands** for low-cost polishing (relevant to Kerala's small clusters)

**Conclusion:** A modern tannery CETP achieves **>99% BOD removal, <2 mg/L Cr, and <1 mg/L sulfide**, complying with CPCB norms.

**Diagram:** Refer Section 4.2 for CETP block architecture.

---

#### **(b) Explain the concept of cleaner production in tanneries. List at least eight process modifications that reduce waste generation at source. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of Cleaner Production (CP) (1 mark):**  
CP is the **continuous application of an integrated preventive environmental strategy** applied to processes, products, and services to increase overall efficiency and reduce risks to humans and the environment. In tanneries, CP targets **raw material conservation, chemical substitution, water economy, and energy efficiency** rather than end-of-pipe treatment.

**Eight Process Modifications (6 marks — 0.75 mark each):**

1. **Hair-saving unhairing** — Use enzymes or low-sulfide (NaHSO₃) unhairing; recover hair as a by-product (fertilizer, felt) instead of dissolving in lime.

2. **Short-float chrome tanning** — Reduce water-to-hide ratio from 100% to 30–50%, increasing Cr concentration → **exhaustion > 90%** → less spent liquor.

3. **Chrome recovery & recycling** — Precipitate spent chrome with MgO, re-dissolve in H₂SO₄; **80% Cr recycling possible**.

4. **Pickleless chrome tanning (Pickle-free)** — Use **high-basicity (66%) chrome** and **masking agents** (sodium formate) to tan at pH 4–5 without pickling → **eliminates salt-laden pickle effluent**.

5. **Sulfide-free or low-sulfide unhairing** — Use oxidative unhairing (H₂O₂ + lime + enzymes) → **eliminates H₂S emission and sulfide wastewater**.

6. **Solvent-free degreasing** — Use aqueous enzymatic degreasing (lipases) instead of perchloroethylene (perc) → **eliminates VOC emission** and health hazard.

7. **Water cascading and counter-current washing** — Reuse wash water from later stages in earlier, less-critical stages → **40–60% water reduction**.

8. **Vegetable/chrome combination tanning** — Use vegetable pre-tan to reduce chrome requirement by 50% → **less Cr waste**.

9. **(Bonus) Fleshings to biogas** — Anaerobic digestion of fleshings in a biogas plant → energy recovery and solid waste volume reduction.

**Conclusion:** CP measures typically reduce tannery water consumption by 50%, Cr usage by 70%, and sludge generation by 60% — a **win-win for industry and environment**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Tanning Industry Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing hide vs skin:** Hide = large animal (cow, buffalo); Skin = small animal (goat, sheep). Examiners deduct **½ mark** for misusing these terms.
> 2. **Wrong chrome formula:** Writing Cr(OH)₂ instead of Cr(OH)₃ — the chromium oxidation state is **+3**, not +2. Cost: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Always write kg/day or mg/L. Numerical answers without units get **0 marks** in KTU ESE.
> 4. **Missing the segregation concept:** A common trap — describing one big ETP for all effluent. Modern KTU questions expect **segregated stream treatment**. Missing this = **lose 2 marks**.
> 5. **Mixing up BOD₅ and COD:** COD > BOD always. COD/BOD ratio in tannery effluent = **1.5–2.0**.
> 6. **Ignoring Kerala-specific context:** Examiners appreciate **Kerala-specific** examples (Kottayam clusters, KSPCB norms, Vembanad Lake) — at least **1 extra mark** for contextual awareness.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Points to Remember (Bullet-Style Rapid Revision):**

- ✅ Tanning = **chemical stabilization of collagen** to prevent putrefaction.
- ✅ **~85% of world leather is chrome-tanned** → chromium management is **the** central environmental issue.
- ✅ Four departments: **Beam-house, Tanyard, Post-tanning, Finishing**.
- ✅ Major effluent pollutants: **BOD, COD, Cr³⁺, S²⁻, NH₃-N, Cl⁻, TDS, color, odour**.
- ✅ Major solid wastes: **fleshings, hair, chrome shavings, buffing dust, sludge**.
- ✅ **Segregation of streams is the first principle** of tannery wastewater treatment.
- ✅ **Chrome recovery** via **MgO precipitation** at pH 8–8.5 → recycle as BCS.
- ✅ **Sulfide oxidation** using air/Mn²⁺ or H₂O₂ to convert S²⁻ to SO₄²⁻.
- ✅ **CPCB discharge norm for total Cr = 2 mg/L**; KSPCB often stricter in sensitive zones.
- ✅ Typical wastewater generation: **25–80 L/kg raw hide**.
- ✅ BOD₅ of raw effluent: **2000–5000 mg/L**; required removal efficiency: **> 99%**.
- ✅ Cleaner production measures can reduce **water use by 50%, Cr by 70%, sludge by 60%**.
- ✅ Kerala has small-scale tannery clusters in **Kottayam, Ernakulam, Thrissur**.
- ✅ Tannery is one of the **17 Red-Category industries** in India.
- ✅ **Chrome sludge = hazardous waste** → must go to **TSDF** (Treatment, Storage, Disposal Facility).
- ✅ **Chromium is trivalent (Cr³⁺) in tannery effluent**, NOT hexavalent (Cr⁶⁺) — but under oxidizing conditions in sludge, **Cr³⁺ can be oxidized to Cr⁶⁺**, which is **carcinogenic** (Group 1 IARC).
- ✅ **ETP sludge is hazardous** under Schedule-II of Hazardous Waste Management Rules.
- ✅ **Pickle-free chrome tanning** is the emerging industry standard for waste minimization.
- ✅ **BOD:COD ratio of tannery effluent ≈ 0.5–0.7**, indicating good biodegradability after sulfide/chrome removal.
- ✅ **Fleshings, hair, and trimmings** can yield **biogas, glue, fertilizer, and pet food** — circular economy opportunities.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dairy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT831 - ENVIRONMENT MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dairy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dairy Industry Environmental Systems**: The dairy industry encompasses the processing of raw milk into products such as pasteurized milk, cheese, butter, yogurt, ice cream, and milk powder. From an Environmental Management Systems (EMS) perspective, it represents a **high-strength, high-volume wastewater generating food processing sector** characterized by significant organic load, suspended solids, fats/oils/greases (FOG), nutrients (nitrogen and phosphorus), and substantial cooling water demand.

**Key Definition:**
**Dairy Effluent** is the wastewater discharged from dairy processing operations, including milk receiving, pasteurization, homogenization, separation, cheese making, packaging, and cleaning-in-place (CIP) operations. It typically exhibits a high **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)** ranging from **1,000 to 4,000 mg/L**, high **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)** of **2,000 to 10,000 mg/L**, and elevated suspended solids.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus**: The syllabus emphasizes how EMS frameworks (like ISO 14001) are applied to manage the unique environmental challenges of the dairy industry — wastewater treatment, solid waste (sludge) handling, energy optimization, and water reuse.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a dairy plant as a **"biological kitchen with industrial plumbing"**. Just like cooking produces greasy dishwater and food scraps, dairy processing produces:
- **Liquid waste (whey, milk residues, cleaning chemicals)** → analogous to greasy sink water but **100x more concentrated** in organic matter.
- **Solid waste (cheese curd trimmings, sludge)** → like vegetable peels but protein-rich.
- **Air emissions (boiler exhaust, refrigeration leaks)** → like kitchen smoke but with refrigerants (CFCs/HFCs) that harm the ozone layer.

The **"environment manager's job"** is to install the equivalent of a smart **dishwasher + compost bin + energy meter** (i.e., Effluent Treatment Plant, Anaerobic Digester, and EMS monitoring system) so that the dairy can operate sustainably — minimizing water pollution, recovering biogas, and recycling water.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Dairy Environmental Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pollutant Parameters (must memorize for KTU exams):**
> - **BOD₅**: **1,000 – 4,000 mg/L** (highly polluting per CPCB norms)
> - **COD**: **2,000 – 10,000 mg/L**
> - **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)**: **300 – 1,500 mg/L**
> - **Fats, Oils & Grease (FOG)**: **100 – 1,000 mg/L**
> - **pH**: **6.5 – 9.5** (variable due to CIP chemicals)
> - **Total Nitrogen**: **20 – 200 mg/L**
> - **Total Phosphorus**: **10 – 100 mg/L**
> - **Wastewater Volume**: **0.5 – 10 m³ per m³ of milk processed**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Wastewater in Dairy Industry

The dairy industry generates pollution from distinct unit operations. Understanding these sources is critical for designing an effective EMS:

### A. Primary Pollution Sources

1. **Milk Receiving and Pasteurization Section**
   - Spillage, leakage, and overflow from tanks
   - High-strength milk residues (BOD ~100,000 mg/L for whole milk)

2. **Cheese and Butter Production**
   - **Whey** — a major pollutant (BOD ~30,000–50,000 mg/L)
   - Whey contains lactose, proteins, and milk salts

3. **Cleaning-In-Place (CIP) Operations**
   - Contributes **30–50%** of total wastewater volume
   - Contains detergents (NaOH), acid cleaners (HNO₃), and sanitizers

4. **Process Water from Equipment Cooling**
   - Heat exchangers, condensers
   - Generally low-strength but high-volume

5. **Sanitary Sewage from Staff Facilities**
   - Standard domestic wastewater characteristics

### B. Solid Waste Sources

- **Sludge** from wastewater treatment (biological sludge)
- **Cheese curd waste**, **buttermilk residue**
- **Packaging waste** (plastic, cardboard, glass)
- **Spent CIP chemicals**

### C. Air Emissions

- **Boiler flue gases** (NOₓ, SO₂, particulates)
- **Refrigerant leaks** (HFCs, ammonia)
- **Odor from wastewater lagoons** (H₂S, volatile fatty acids)

## 2.2 Why EMS is Critical in Dairy Industry

> [!NOTE]
> The dairy sector ranks among the **top 5 polluting food industries** globally in terms of organic load discharged to water bodies. Per the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)**, India, dairy effluents must meet the following discharge standards:

| Parameter | CPCB Discharge Limit (mg/L, except pH) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **pH** | **6.5 – 8.5** |
| **BOD₅ (3 days, 27°C)** | **≤ 100 mg/L** |
| **COD** | **≤ 250 mg/L** |
| **TSS** | **≤ 100 mg/L** |
| **FOG** | **≤ 10 mg/L** |
| **Total Nitrogen** | **≤ 10 mg/L** |

Failure to comply triggers regulatory penalties under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Board Examinations**

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Organic Load** | $\text{OL} = Q \times \text{BOD}$ | $Q$ = wastewater flow (m³/day); BOD in mg/L; result in kg/day |
| **Population Equivalent (PE)** | $\text{PE} = \dfrac{Q \times \text{BOD}}{60}$ | Standard per capita BOD = **60 g/day** |
| **F/M Ratio** | $\text{F/M} = \dfrac{S_{0}}{\theta_{c} \cdot X}$ | Substrate-to-microorganism ratio (dimensionless) |
| **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** | $\theta_{H} = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | $V$ = reactor volume (m³); $\theta_H$ in days |
| **Solids Retention Time (SRT)** | $\theta_{c} = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_{w} \cdot X_{r} + Q_{e} \cdot X_{e}}$ | Critical for activated sludge design |
| **BOD Removal Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_{\text{in}} - \text{BOD}_{\text{out}}}{\text{BOD}_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | Expressed as percentage |
| **Biogas Yield (Anaerobic)** | $Y_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.35 \times \left(\text{COD}_{\text{removed}}\right) \times V$ | m³ CH₄ produced per day |
| **Volumetric Loading Rate (VLR)** | $\text{VLR} = \dfrac{Q \times \text{COD}}{V}$ | kg COD/m³·day |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Conversion Logic**: $\text{1 mg/L} = \text{1 g/m}^3 = \text{1 ppm (in dilute aqueous solutions)}$. Multiply by flow $Q$ in m³/day to convert concentration (mg/L) to mass load (kg/day).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The dairy EMS integrates three pillars of sustainability:

1. **Environmental Protection** — Preventing water body eutrophication and soil contamination.
2. **Resource Recovery** — Whey protein recovery (~$500–1,500/ton), biogas production (~$0.50/m³), water reuse.
3. **Regulatory Compliance** — Avoiding CPCB/SPCB penalties and maintaining ISO 14001 certification.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Population Equivalent of a Dairy Plant

The **Population Equivalent (PE)** is a fundamental metric used in KTU exams to express industrial organic load in terms of equivalent domestic population.

### Step 1: Identify Given Data
Let a dairy plant process **$Q = 500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$** of milk and generate wastewater with **$\text{BOD}_{5} = 2{,}500 \text{ mg/L}$**.

### Step 2: Convert Concentration to Standard Units
Since standard per capita BOD is given in g/day, convert mg/L to g/m³:

$$
\text{BOD}_{5} = 2{,}500 \text{ mg/L} = 2{,}500 \text{ g/m}^3
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Total Daily BOD Load

$$
\text{OL} = Q \times \text{BOD}_{5} = 500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 2{,}500 \text{ g/m}^3
$$

$$
\text{OL} = 1{,}250{,}000 \text{ g/day} = 1{,}250 \text{ kg/day}
$$

### Step 4: Apply PE Formula

$$
\text{PE} = \dfrac{\text{OL}}{\text{Per capita BOD}} = \dfrac{1{,}250{,}000 \text{ g/day}}{60 \text{ g/capita·day}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PE} \approx 20{,}833 \text{ persons equivalent}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: This single dairy plant pollutes equivalent to a town of ~20,833 people — explaining why dairy effluents require dedicated treatment plants rather than municipal sewers alone.

## 3.2 Derivation: Anaerobic Digester Biogas Yield

Anaerobic digestion is a **KTU-favorite topic**. We derive methane production from COD destruction.

### Step 1: Establish Stoichiometric Basis
The Buswell equation (simplified) gives the theoretical methane yield per kg of COD destroyed:

$$
\text{CH}_4 \text{ yield} = 0.35 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 \text{ per kg COD destroyed}
$$

### Step 2: Identify Operating Parameters
- Influent COD: $\text{COD}_{\text{in}} = 4{,}000 \text{ mg/L} = 4 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Effluent COD: $\text{COD}_{\text{out}} = 800 \text{ mg/L} = 0.8 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Flow rate: $Q = 200 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$
- Reactor volume: $V = 1{,}000 \text{ m}^3$

### Step 3: Compute COD Removal Efficiency

$$
\eta_{\text{COD}} = \dfrac{4{,}000 - 800}{4{,}000} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

### Step 4: Calculate Daily COD Mass Removed

$$
\text{COD}_{\text{removed}} = Q \times (\text{COD}_{\text{in}} - \text{COD}_{\text{out}})
$$

$$
\text{COD}_{\text{removed}} = 200 \times (4 - 0.8) = 200 \times 3.2 = 640 \text{ kg/day}
$$

### Step 5: Compute Daily Methane Volume

$$
V_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.35 \times 640 = 224 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4/\text{day}
$$

### Step 6: Compute Volumetric Loading Rate (VLR) — Verification

$$
\text{VLR} = \dfrac{Q \times \text{COD}_{\text{in}}}{V} = \dfrac{200 \times 4}{1{,}000} = 0.8 \text{ kg COD/m}^3\text{·day}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight**: A VLR of **0.8 kg COD/m³·day** falls within the typical mesophilic anaerobic digester range of **0.5 – 3.0 kg COD/m³·day**, confirming safe operational design.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Dairy Industry Process Flow with Environmental Interfaces

The following block diagram illustrates how dairy unit operations interact with waste streams and EMS controls:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Raw Milk Receiving] --> B[Pre-treatment and Pasteurization]
    B --> C[Separation Cream and Skim Milk]
    C --> D[Cheese Making Section]
    C --> E[Butter Production]
    C --> F[Liquid Milk Packaging]
    D --> G[Whey Collection Tank]
    G --> G1[Whey Protein Recovery Unit]
    G1 --> G2[Byproduct to Market]
    B --> H[Cleaning in Place CIP Loop]
    H --> H1[CIP Wastewater Tank]
    F --> I[Process Cooling Water]
    I --> I1[Cooling Tower Blowdown]
    H1 --> J[Equalization Tank]
    I1 --> J
    E --> J
    D --> J
    J --> K[Screening and Grit Removal]
    K --> L[Fat Trap DAF]
    L --> M[Anaerobic Digester UASB]
    M --> N[Aerobic Treatment Activated Sludge]
    N --> O[Secondary Clarifier]
    O --> P[Tertiary Treatment Polishing]
    P --> Q[Treated Effluent Discharge or Reuse]
    L --> R[Skimmings to Biogas]
    M --> R
    R --> S[Biogas Holder]
    S --> T[Boiler Fuel]
    O --> U[Sludge Thickener]
    U --> V[Sludge Drying Beds]
    V --> W[Composted Sludge Agricultural Use]
    A --> X[EMS Monitoring ISO 14001]
    X --> Y[Compliance Reporting to CPCB SPCB]
    Y --> Z[Continuous Improvement PDCA Cycle]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Dairy EMS Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Inputs Raw Milk, Water, Energy] --> nodeB[Process Operations Dairy Plant]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Outputs Product and Waste]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Wastewater Stream]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Solid Waste Stream]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Air Emissions]
    nodeD --> nodeG[ETP Treatment Train]
    nodeG --> nodeG1[Primary Clarifier]
    nodeG1 --> nodeG2[UASB Reactor]
    nodeG2 --> nodeG3[Activated Sludge]
    nodeG3 --> nodeH[Quality Check BOD COD TSS pH]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Compliant Discharge]
    nodeE --> nodeJ[Sludge Management]
    nodeJ --> nodeJ1[Thickening]
    nodeJ1 --> nodeJ2[Dewatering]
    nodeJ2 --> nodeJ3[Composting]
    nodeF --> nodeK[Stack Monitoring]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Emission Inventory]
    nodeH --> nodeM[EMS Performance Indicators]
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeJ3 --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN[Management Review]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Corrective Actions]
    nodeO --> nodeB
```

## 4.3 Treatment Train Hierarchy

| Stage | Unit Operation | Function | Typical Removal |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Preliminary** | Bar Screen, Grit Chamber | Remove large solids | Physical debris |
| **Primary** | Equalization Tank, Fat Trap (DAF) | Flow & load balancing, FOG removal | **40–60% FOG**, **20–30% BOD** |
| **Secondary (Anaerobic)** | UASB / Anaerobic Filter | High-rate organic destruction, biogas | **60–80% COD** |
| **Secondary (Aerobic)** | Activated Sludge / SBR | Polishing, nitrification | **80–95% BOD** |
| **Tertiary** | Filtration, Disinfection | Final polishing | TSS polishing, pathogen kill |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List **four major environmental pollutants** generated by the dairy industry and state their typical concentration range.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD₅)**: **1,000 – 4,000 mg/L**
2. **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)**: **2,000 – 10,000 mg/L**
3. **Fats, Oils & Grease (FOG)**: **100 – 1,000 mg/L**
4. **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)**: **300 – 1,500 mg/L**

These pollutants cause oxygen depletion in receiving water bodies, surface scum formation, and eutrophication.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is **Cleaning-In-Place (CIP)** wastewater and why is it environmentally significant?

**Model Answer:**
**CIP wastewater** is the spent cleaning solution generated when dairy pipelines, tanks, and processing equipment are sanitized using hot water, caustic soda (NaOH), nitric acid (HNO₃), and sanitizers. It is environmentally significant because:
- It contributes **30–50%** of the total wastewater volume in a dairy plant.
- It contains **high pH (10–12)** and **low pH (2–3)** alternating streams requiring neutralization.
- It carries residual milk solids, contributing to **organic load**.
- Improper disposal can disrupt biological treatment systems due to pH shock.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** (a) Describe the **sources and characteristics of wastewater** generated in a dairy industry. (7 marks)

**(b) Design a suitable **treatment scheme** for dairy effluent with a flow of **$300 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$** and **BOD = 2,000 mg/L**, justifying each unit. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Sources of Dairy Wastewater:**

1. **Milk Receiving & Pasteurization** — spillage, leakage from tanks; highly concentrated milk residues (BOD ~100,000 mg/L).
2. **Cheese & Butter Production** — **whey** discharge (BOD ~30,000–50,000 mg/L); buttermilk residue.
3. **CIP Operations** — alkaline and acidic cleaning solutions; contributes **30–50%** of volume.
4. **Process Cooling Water** — heat exchanger discharge; high volume but low strength.
5. **Sanitary Sewage** — from staff toilets, canteens.

**Characteristics Summary:**

| Parameter | Typical Range |
| :--- | :--- |
| pH | 6.5 – 9.5 |
| BOD₅ | 1,000 – 4,000 mg/L |
| COD | 2,000 – 10,000 mg/L |
| TSS | 300 – 1,500 mg/L |
| FOG | 100 – 1,000 mg/L |
| Total Nitrogen | 20 – 200 mg/L |
| Total Phosphorus | 10 – 100 mg/L |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Design Calculation:**

Step 1: Compute organic load.

$$
\text{OL} = 300 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 2{,}000 \text{ g/m}^3 = 600{,}000 \text{ g/day} = 600 \text{ kg BOD/day}
$$

Step 2: Compute population equivalent.

$$
\text{PE} = \dfrac{600{,}000}{60} = 10{,}000 \text{ persons equivalent}
$$

Step 3: Select treatment train.

| Unit | Justification |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Bar Screen + Grit Chamber** | Remove coarse solids; protect downstream pumps |
| **Equalization Tank** | Dampen pH fluctuations from CIP; buffer flow |
| **Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF)** | Remove **FOG** (critical; otherwise clogs aerators) |
| **UASB Reactor** | High-strength BOD removal with **biogas recovery** |
| **Activated Sludge (ASP)** | Achieve BOD < 30 mg/L; nitrification |
| **Secondary Clarifier** | Solid-liquid separation |
| **Tertiary Filtration + Chlorination** | Meet CPCB discharge norms |

Step 4: Verify effluent compliance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> After UASB + ASP, expected BOD reduction: **80% (anaerobic) + 90% (aerobic) ≈ 98% combined**, giving outlet BOD ≈ **40 mg/L** → **compliant with CPCB ≤ 100 mg/L** standard.

---

### Question B — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** (a) Explain the concept of **Population Equivalent (PE)**. A dairy plant discharges **$400 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$** of wastewater with **BOD = 3,000 mg/L**. Calculate the **PE**. (7 marks)

**(b) Discuss the role of **Anaerobic Digestion (UASB)** in dairy wastewater treatment with a neat sketch. List its advantages. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Concept:**
**Population Equivalent (PE)** is the imaginary population that would produce the same organic load (BOD) as the industrial discharge, assuming a standard per capita BOD contribution of **60 g/capita·day**.

**Calculation:**

Step 1: Convert BOD to load.

$$
\text{OL} = 400 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 3{,}000 \text{ g/m}^3 = 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ g/day}
$$

Step 2: Apply PE formula.

$$
\text{PE} = \dfrac{1{,}200{,}000}{60} = \boxed{20{,}000 \text{ persons equivalent}}
$$

**Interpretation:** This single dairy plant exerts organic pollution equivalent to a residential colony of 20,000 people.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**UASB Reactor Working Principle:**
