The **Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB)** reactor is a high-rate anaerobic treatment unit. Wastewater flows **upward** through a dense blanket of **granular anaerobic sludge**. Microorganisms convert organic matter into **biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)**. The three-phase separator at the top retains sludge and captures biogas.

**Functional Sketch:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Influent Dairy Wastewater] --> P2[Distribution Inlet]
    P2 --> P3[Sludge Blanket Zone]
    P3 --> P4[Three Phase Separator]
    P4 --> P5[Treated Effluent Outlet]
    P4 --> P6[Biogas Collection Dome]
    P3 --> P7[Sludge Recirculation]
    P7 --> P3
```

**Advantages of UASB for Dairy Effluent:**

1. **High organic removal efficiency** — **70–85% COD reduction**.
2. **Biogas recovery** — renewable energy for boilers (~$0.50/m³).
3. **Low energy input** — no aeration required (unlike ASP).
4. **Low sludge yield** — minimizes sludge disposal cost.
5. **Compact footprint** — suitable for space-constrained urban dairies.
6. **Tolerates high organic loads** — VLR up to **10–15 kg COD/m³·day**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Dairy EMS Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors**: Students often confuse **mg/L with g/m³** or forget to multiply flow × concentration. **Always show unit cancellation.**
> 2. **Confusing BOD with COD**: BOD is biodegradable fraction; COD includes both. Never equate them.
> 3. **Skipping Justification**: KTU examiners deduct marks if you list treatment units without explaining **why** each is selected.
> 4. **Forgetting FOG Removal**: Many answers jump to biological treatment without addressing **FOG removal via DAF** — a critical dairy-specific step.
> 5. **Incorrect PE Formula**: Some students use **BOD = 200 g/capita** (sewage basis) instead of **60 g/capita** (Indian standard). Verify the assumed per capita value.
> 6. **Missing CPCB Standards**: Always quote discharge norms (e.g., **BOD ≤ 100 mg/L**) to demonstrate regulatory awareness.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Dairy & EMS (Module 3)**

- **Dairy effluents are HIGH-STRENGTH organic wastewaters** — BOD typically **1,000–4,000 mg/L**; COD **2,000–10,000 mg/L**.
- **Whey** is the single most polluting byproduct (BOD ~30,000–50,000 mg/L) — must be recovered, not discharged.
- **CIP wastewater** contributes **30–50%** of plant volume — requires neutralization before biological treatment.
- **FOG removal (via DAF)** is a mandatory preliminary step in dairy ETP design.
- **Treatment Train Sequence**: Screening → Equalization → DAF (FOG removal) → UASB (anaerobic) → ASP (aerobic) → Tertiary → Disinfection.
- **Population Equivalent Formula**: $\text{PE} = \dfrac{Q \times \text{BOD}}{60}$ — use **60 g/capita·day** (Indian standard).
- **UASB reactor** is preferred for dairy due to high strength, biogas recovery, and low energy demand.
- **CPCB Discharge Standards**: BOD ≤ 100 mg/L; COD ≤ 250 mg/L; TSS ≤ 100 mg/L; FOG ≤ 10 mg/L; pH 6.5–8.5.
- **EMS Framework (ISO 14001)**: Plan → Do → Check → Act (PDCA cycle) for continuous environmental improvement.
- **Biogas yield from anaerobic digestion**: approximately **0.35 m³ CH₄ per kg COD destroyed**.
- **Resource Recovery Hierarchy**: Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover energy → Dispose (apply to whey, sludge, packaging).
- **Key Indian Regulations**: Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, Hazardous Waste Rules, E-Waste Rules — relevant to dairy EMS compliance.
- **Cooling water** discharge is high-volume but low-strength; can often be recycled directly with minimal treatment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cement and Chemical Industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT831 - ENVIRONMENT MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cement and Chemical Industries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 The Cement Industry — Definition & Scope

The **cement industry** is classified as one of the most resource-intensive and emission-heavy heavy industrial sectors under KTU's environmental management framework. It is broadly categorized as a **"High Mass, High Energy, High Emission (HHE)"** sector because of its colossal consumption of virgin raw materials, fossil fuel energy, and its significant contribution to global anthropogenic CO₂ emissions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *Cement industry is a continuous-process heavy manufacturing industry that converts calcareous (limestone/chalk) and argillaceous (clay/shale) raw materials into hydraulic binding agents (clinker → cement) through pyroprocessing at temperatures exceeding 1450 °C, and is recognized under Schedule-I of Indian environmental regulation as a "Red Category" industry due to its high pollution potential.*

### Components of Hydraulic Cement

| Component | Chemical Formula | Role in Cement | Approximate % by Mass |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tricalcium Silicate (Alite) | Ca₃SiO₅ (C₃S) | Early strength development | **45–60 %** |
| Dicalcium Silicate (Belite) | Ca₂SiO₄ (C₂S) | Long-term strength | **15–30 %** |
| Tricalcium Aluminate | Ca₃Al₂O₆ (C₃A) | Flash setting control | **6–12 %** |
| Tetracalcium Aluminoferrite | Ca₄Al₂Fe₂O₁₀ (C₄AF) | Color and minor strength | **6–8 %** |

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Cake Baking" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Think of cement manufacturing exactly like baking a layered cake in a giant industrial oven. The **limestone is the flour (bulk ingredient)**, **clay is the cocoa powder (adds character/flavor)**, the **rotary kiln is the oven (heats everything to 1450 °C)**, and the **clinker coming out is the baked cake**. Just as you cannot un-bake a cake, the chemical reactions in the kiln are *irreversible pyro-metallurgical transformations*. If the oven temperature drops, the cake collapses — similarly, if the kiln cools, you don't get cement, you get wasted raw mix. The "frosting" added later is **gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O)**, which controls setting time. The waste from the oven (dust + gases) is exactly what an Environmental Management System (EMS) like ISO 14001 has to capture and control.

---

## 1.2 The Chemical Industry — Definition & Scope

The **chemical industry** is an umbrella sector encompassing the production of industrial chemicals, petrochemicals, pharmaceuticals, agro-chemicals, fertilizers, polymers, dyes, acids, and alkalis. It is the **"backbone sector"** of modern civilization because virtually every other industry (textiles, agriculture, healthcare, electronics) is downstream-dependent on chemical outputs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *A chemical industry is an establishment carrying out the manufacture, conversion, formulation, or packaging of organic or inorganic chemicals through batch/continuous/ semi-batch processes, involving unit operations such as reaction, separation, distillation, crystallization, and drying, and governed under the Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC Rules, 1989) and the Factories Act, 1948.*

### Classification of Chemical Industries (KTU Board Standard)

| S.No | Sub-Sector | Example Product | Key Environmental Concern |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heavy Inorganic Chemicals | Sulfuric acid, Chlor-alkali | Mercury (Hg), Chlorine gas |
| 2 | Petrochemicals | Ethylene, Benzene | VOC, PAHs |
| 3 | Fertilizers | Urea, DAP, Ammonia | NOₓ, NH₃, Phosphogypsum |
| 4 | Pharmaceuticals | APIs, Tablets | Drug residues, Solvents |
| 5 | Pesticides/Agro-chemicals | Endosulfan, DDT | Persistent Organic Pollutants |
| 6 | Dyes & Pigments | Azo dyes | Color, Heavy metals |
| 7 | Polymers/Plastics | PVC, LDPE | Microplastics, Monomers |

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Chemistry" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> A chemical industry is essentially a **scaled-up, highly instrumented chemistry laboratory kitchen**. Imagine a household kitchen where you are making multiple dishes (soups, curries, baked goods) simultaneously using stoves, pressure cookers, refrigerators, and mixers — each emitting steam, smoke, and residues. Now multiply that kitchen by 10,000 in scale and add toxic reagents. The **"stove"** is the **reactor**, the **"chimney"** is the **emission stack**, the **"sink"** is the **effluent treatment plant (ETP)**, and the **"dustbin"** is the **hazardous waste storage facility**. Just as a poorly managed home kitchen leads to pest infestation and food poisoning, a poorly managed chemical plant leads to **Bhopal-type disasters (1984)** and chronic pollution.

---

## 1.3 Why These Industries Sit at the Heart of Environmental Management

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Context:**
> Cement and Chemical Industries together contribute **~14 % of global industrial CO₂ emissions** and **~25 % of global industrial hazardous waste generation**. Any discussion of environmental management systems (EMS) that bypasses these two sectors is incomplete — they are explicitly listed under **17 Highly Polluting Industries (HPI)** of the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), India.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Cement Manufacturing — Process Flow & Environmental Interface

### 2.1.1 The Six Major Process Stages

The cement manufacturing process can be deconstructed into six discrete, environmentally sensitive stages:

**Stage 1 — Quarrying & Raw Material Extraction**
- Open-pit mining of limestone (CaCO₃), clay, sand, and iron ore.
- Environmental burden: **Land degradation, dust generation, biodiversity loss, blasting noise**.

**Stage 2 — Raw Material Crushing & Pre-blending**
- Jaw crushers, hammer mills reduce limestone to < 80 mm.
- Blending silos homogenize the raw mix.
- Environmental burden: **Particulate Matter (PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅) emissions**.

**Stage 3 — Pyroprocessing in the Rotary Kiln**
- This is the *thermodynamic heart* of the cement plant.
- Reactions occur in five sequential sub-stages:
  - **Drying zone (≤ 200 °C):** Free moisture evaporation.
  - **Preheating zone (200–600 °C):** Clay dehydration (loss of structural water).
  - **Calcination zone (600–900 °C):** Limestone dissociation.
  - **Clinkering zone (1400–1500 °C):** B熟料 formation (C₃S, C₂S, C₃A, C₄AF).
  - **Cooling zone (1500 → 100 °C):** Rapid quench to preserve reactive phases.

**Stage 4 — Clinker Cooling & Storage**
- Grate coolers or planetary coolers.
- Waste Heat Recovery (WHR) systems recover up to **30 % of input energy**.

**Stage 5 — Cement Grinding & Blending**
- Ball mills, vertical roller mills.
- Gypsum (3–5 %) added to control setting time.
- Environmental burden: **High PM emissions, high electricity consumption**.

**Stage 6 — Packing & Dispatch**
- Pneumatic conveyors, automatic packers.

### 2.1.2 Key Stoichiometric Reactions

The **heart of the calcination reaction** (also called the "burning reaction"):

$$\text{CaCO}_3(s) \xrightarrow{\Delta \, (\approx 900^\circ \text{C})} \text{CaO}(s) + \text{CO}_2(g) \uparrow$$

This single reaction is responsible for **~60 %** of the CO₂ emissions from a cement plant because limestone releases its own CO₂ in addition to the combustion CO₂ from fuel.

The **clinker formation reaction** (Bogue's reaction):

$$\text{2 CaO} \cdot \text{SiO}_2 + \text{CaO} \rightarrow \text{Ca}_3\text{SiO}_5 \, (\text{C}_3\text{S})$$

$$\text{3 CaO} \cdot \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{CaO} + \text{SiO}_2 \rightarrow \text{Ca}_4\text{Al}_2\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_{10} \, (\text{C}_4\text{AF})$$

### 2.1.3 Environmental Emissions Inventory

| Emission Stream | Major Pollutants | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Stack Gases | CO₂, NOₓ, SO₂, PM, CO, HF, HCl | **0.8–1.0 t CO₂ per tonne clinker** |
| Particulate Matter | PM₁₀, PM₂.₅ from kilns & coolers | **0.1–0.5 kg/t clinker** |
| Noise | Kiln, crushers, mills | **85–110 dB(A)** |
| Solid Waste | Kiln dust, mill rejects | **5–15 % of raw feed** |
| Water Effluent | Cooling tower blowdown, runoff | High pH, suspended solids |

---

## 2.2 Chemical Industry — Process Categories & Environmental Interface

### 2.2.1 Generic Chemical Process Flow

Every chemical plant — whether it is producing sulfuric acid or aspirin — follows a generic five-stage flow:

**Stage 1 — Feedstock Preparation**
Purification of raw materials (e.g., sulfur for H₂SO₄, naphtha for petrochemicals).

**Stage 2 — Reaction (Reactor)**
- Batch reactor (pharma, dyes)
- Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor — CSTR (bulk chemicals)
- Plug Flow Reactor — PFR (petrochemicals)
- Fluidized Bed Reactor (catalytic cracking)

**Stage 3 — Separation**
- Distillation, extraction, absorption, crystallization, membrane separation.

**Stage 4 — Purification & Formulation**
- Final polishing, additive blending, stabilization.

**Stage 5 — Packaging & Storage**

### 2.2.2 Major Unit Operations & Their Environmental Footprint

| Unit Operation | Function | Typical Pollutant Released |
|---|---|---|
| Distillation Column | Separation by volatility | VOC emissions, condenser leaks |
| Absorber/Scrubber | Gas-liquid mass transfer | Spent solvent/lime slurry |
| Drying (Spray/Drum) | Moisture removal | PM, VOC |
| Electrolysis (Chlor-alkali) | NaCl → Cl₂ + NaOH + H₂ | Hg (older plants), Cl₂ gas |
| Cracking Furnace | Break long hydrocarbons | CO, NOₓ, unburned HC |

### 2.2.3 Fertilizer Industry — Special Focus

The **fertilizer industry** is the largest sub-segment of the chemical industry in India and is highly relevant to KTU assessments.

**Haber–Bosch Process for Ammonia:**

$$N_2(g) + 3H_2(g) \xrightleftharpoons{\text{Fe catalyst, } 400-500^\circ\text{C, 200-300 atm}} 2NH_3(g) + \text{Heat}$$

**Ostwald Process for Nitric Acid (Industrial Production):**

$$4NH_3 + 5O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt/Rh catalyst}} 4NO + 6H_2O$$

$$2NO + O_2 \rightarrow 2NO_2$$

$$3NO_2 + H_2O \rightarrow 2HNO_3 + NO$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Environmental Linkage:**
> The fertilizer industry is the largest industrial source of **NOₓ emissions** in India and produces massive quantities of **phosphogypsum** (~5 tonnes per tonne of phosphoric acid), a Category-A hazardous waste.

---

## 2.3 Pollution Control Technologies (KTU Board Standard List)

### 2.3.1 Air Pollution Control Devices (APCDs)

| Device | Mechanism | Target Pollutant | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cyclone Separator | Centrifugal inertia | PM (coarse) | **70–90 %** |
| Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) | Electrostatic charging | PM (fine) | **>99.5 %** |
| Baghouse/Fabric Filter | Filtration | PM (fine) | **>99.9 %** |
| Wet Scrubber (Venturi) | Gas-liquid absorption | SO₂, HCl, HF | **80–95 %** |
| Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) | NH₃ + NOₓ → N₂ + H₂O | NOₓ | **70–95 %** |
| Selective Non-Catalytic Reduction (SNCR) | Thermal reduction | NOₓ | **30–70 %** |

### 2.3.2 Water Pollution Control Sequence

| Stage | Treatment | Typical Removal |
|---|---|---|
| Primary | Screening, Sedimentation | SS, Oil-Grease |
| Secondary | Activated Sludge / ASP | BOD, COD |
| Tertiary | RO, Ion-exchange, Adsorption | Heavy metals, TDS |
| Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD) | Evaporation + Crystallization | Salt recovery |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| S.No | Formula / Concept | Equation / Statement | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Calcination CO₂ generation | $$\text{Mass CO}_2 = \left(\frac{44}{100}\right) \times \text{Mass of CaCO}_3 \text{ calcined}$$ | Stoichiometric CO₂ estimation |
| 2 | Stoichiometric Air Requirement (SAR) | $$\text{SAR} = \frac{\text{Mass O}_2 \text{ required}}{\text{Mass O}_2 \text{ in air}} = \frac{\text{O}_2 \text{ needed}}{0.232}$$ | Combustion calculations |
| 3 | Excess Air Ratio (EA) | $$\text{EA (\%)} = \frac{\text{Actual Air} - \text{Stoichiometric Air}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air}} \times 100$$ | Burner tuning |
| 4 | Particulate Collection Efficiency | $$\eta = \frac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100$$ | ESP/Cyclone rating |
| 5 | Conversion in PFR | $$X = 1 - \exp(-k\tau)$$ | Reactor design |
| 6 | Conversion in CSTR | $$X = \frac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau}$$ | Reactor design |
| 7 | BOD Dilution Formula | $$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \times f}{P}$$ | Wastewater characterization |
| 8 | Specific Energy Consumption (Cement) | $$\text{SEC} = \frac{\text{Total energy (MJ)}}{\text{Tonnes of cement}}$$ | Plant benchmarking |
| 9 | Carbon Intensity (Cement) | $$\text{CI} = \frac{\text{CO}_2 \text{ emissions (t)}}{\text{Clinker produced (t)}}$$ | Sustainability KPI |
| 10 | Theoretical Heat of Clinkerization | $$Q_{\text{theor}} \approx 1700 \text{ kJ/kg clinker}$$ | Energy audit baseline |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivations Required for KTU Board Examinations

### 3.1.1 Stoichiometric CO₂ Emissions from a Cement Plant — Full Derivation

**Problem Statement:** *A cement plant produces 5000 tonnes/day of Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC). The clinker-to-cement ratio is 0.95, and the limestone (CaCO₃) stoichiometric purity is 92 %. Estimate the daily CO₂ emissions arising purely from calcination, ignoring fuel-related combustion emissions.*

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data and Required Output**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Daily OPC production} &= 5000 \, \text{t/day} \\
\text{Clinker-to-cement ratio (CCR)} &= 0.95 \\
\text{Limestone purity (CaCO}_3 \text{ fraction)} &= 0.92 \\
\text{Molar mass of CaCO}_3 &= 100 \, \text{g/mol} \\
\text{Molar mass of CO}_2 &= 44 \, \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Daily Clinker Production**

Since OPC contains 95 % clinker, the daily clinker output is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Clinker produced} &= \text{OPC production} \times \text{CCR} \\
&= 5000 \times 0.95 \\
&= 4750 \, \text{t/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Relate Clinker to Lime (CaO) Requirement**

The Bogue calculation tells us that to produce 1 tonne of clinker, approximately **0.65 tonnes of CaO** is required (since CaO constitutes ~65 % of clinker mass by stoichiometry).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CaO required per day} &= 4750 \times 0.65 \\
&= 3087.5 \, \text{t/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert CaO Requirement to Equivalent Pure CaCO₃**

The molar masses tell us: 100 g CaCO₃ produces 56 g CaO.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of pure CaCO}_3 \text{ required} &= \text{Mass CaO} \times \frac{100}{56} \\
&= 3087.5 \times \frac{100}{56} \\
&= 5513.39 \, \text{t/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Adjust for Limestone Purity (92 % CaCO₃)**

Since limestone is only 92 % pure CaCO₃, we need more raw limestone:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Actual limestone required} &= \frac{5513.39}{0.92} \\
&= 5992.82 \, \text{t/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate Pure CaCO₃ Input Daily**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pure CaCO}_3 \text{ fed daily} &= 5992.82 \times 0.92 \\
&= 5513.39 \, \text{t/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply the Stoichiometric Mass Ratio for CO₂**

For every 100 kg of pure CaCO₃ calcined, 44 kg of CO₂ is liberated:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CO}_2 \text{ emitted per day} &= 5513.39 \times \frac{44}{100} \\
&= 2425.89 \, \text{t CO}_2 \text{/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Annualize the Emission**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual CO}_2 \text{ (operating 330 days)} &= 2425.89 \times 330 \\
&= 800544 \, \text{t CO}_2 \text{/year} \\
&\approx 0.80 \, \text{Million tonnes CO}_2 \text{/year (MtCO}_2 \text{e)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Compute Carbon Intensity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CI} &= \frac{\text{CO}_2}{\text{Clinker}} = \frac{2425.89}{4750} \\
&= 0.511 \, \text{t CO}_2 \text{/t clinker}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> The typical industry benchmark is **0.85–0.95 t CO₂/t clinker**. Our value of 0.51 is lower because we excluded fuel combustion emissions (which contribute the remaining ~0.4 t CO₂/t clinker). This is a classic KTU numerical that tests students' ability to separate *process emissions* from *energy emissions*.

---

### 3.1.2 ESP Collection Efficiency — Worked Derivation

**Problem Statement:** *An ESP treats 200,000 Nm³/hr of flue gas at a cement kiln. The inlet dust concentration is 12,000 mg/Nm³ and the outlet is 75 mg/Nm³. Calculate (a) the collection efficiency, and (b) the daily mass of dust captured.*

**Step 1 — Collection Efficiency Formula**

$$
\eta = \frac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{12000 - 75}{12000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{11925}{12000} \times 100 \\
&= 99.375 \, \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily Mass of Flue Gas**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Daily volume} &= 200{,}000 \times 24 \\
&= 4{,}800{,}000 \, \text{Nm}^3\text{/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass Captured = Mass In − Mass Out**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass captured per Nm}^3 &= C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}} \\
&= 12000 - 75 \\
&= 11925 \, \text{mg/Nm}^3 \\
&= 0.011925 \, \text{kg/Nm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Daily mass captured} &= 0.011925 \times 4{,}800{,}000 \\
&= 57{,}240 \, \text{kg/day} \\
&\approx 57.24 \, \text{t/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.1.3 Reactor Conversion — PFR vs. CSTR (Comparison)

**Given:** First-order reaction, $k = 0.05 \, \text{min}^{-1}$, space time $\tau = 30 \, \text{min}$.

**For a Plug Flow Reactor (PFR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{\text{PFR}} &= 1 - \exp(-k\tau) \\
&= 1 - \exp(-0.05 \times 30) \\
&= 1 - \exp(-1.5) \\
&= 1 - 0.2231 \\
&= 0.7769 \approx 77.7 \, \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**For a Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{\text{CSTR}} &= \frac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau} \\
&= \frac{0.05 \times 30}{1 + 0.05 \times 30} \\
&= \frac{1.5}{2.5} \\
&= 0.6 = 60 \, \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Insight:**
> PFR always gives higher conversion than CSTR for the same volume when reaction order is positive. This is a recurring KTU numerical.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Pollution Source × Control Technology

| Industry | Pollutant | Source | Primary Control | Secondary Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cement | PM₁₀, PM₂.₅ | Kiln stack, crusher | ESP / Baghouse | Cyclone pre-cleaner |
| Cement | NOₓ | Kiln (thermal) | SNCR / SCR | Low-NOₓ burner |
| Cement | SO₂ | Kiln (raw mix sulfides) | Wet scrubber | Switch to low-S fuel |
| Cement | CO₂ | Calcination + fuel | Alternative fuels | CCUS (emerging) |
| Chemical | VOC | Storage, reactor vent | Condenser + Adsorption | Thermal oxidizer |
| Chemical | Cl₂ gas | Chlor-alkali cell | NaOH scrubber | Leak detection (LDS) |
| Chemical | Hg | Older chlor-alkali plants | Activated carbon | Membrane cell conversion |
| Fertilizer | NH₃ | Urea prilling | Acid scrubber (H₂SO₄) | Wet scrubber |
| Fertilizer | NOₓ | Nitric acid plant | SCR | Low-NOₓ burner |
